title: On the equivalence of semi-discrete Active Flux and Discontinuous Galerkin methods and a comparison of their performance
summary: The Active Flux (AF) method employs a globally continuous approximation, like continuous Finite Element methods. This is achieved through the placement of point values at cell interfaces which are shared between adjacent cells. With, on average, K+1 degrees of freedom per cell, Active Flux achieves a polynomial approximation of degree K+1, while the Discontinuous Galerkin (DG) method uses only polynomials of degree K, i.e. one degree less with the same number of degrees of freedom. Despite all the differences, in this paper we show, however, that for linear problems in one and several dimensions as well as -- in some sense -- for nonlinear ones, semi-discrete AF and DG are the same method. We identify a mapping between their respective degrees of freedom, upon which the updates of these degrees of freedom turn out to agree. On the one hand, AF therefore seems more economical then DG for a given value of the error, and we confirm this in numerical experiments. On the other hand, this is a way to understand superconvergence of DG in a natural way, and we show how Radau polynomials and their zeros appear in the mapping between DG and AF: In the Radau points, AF "shines through" as the background high-order scheme behind DG.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Active Flux (AF) and Discontinuous Galerkin (DG) methods are mathematically equivalent for linear problems and in some sense for nonlinear ones, despite AF achieving higher polynomial degree with same degrees of freedom.
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between Active Flux and Discontinuous Galerkin methods, both popular high-order numerical methods, and explain why AF appears more economical while DG exhibits superconvergence properties.
AI.method: The authors identify a mathematical mapping between the degrees of freedom of AF and DG methods, showing that their semi-discrete formulations are equivalent for linear problems in one and multiple dimensions, and extend this analysis to nonlinear problems.
AI.result: AF achieves polynomial approximation of degree K+1 with K+1 degrees of freedom per cell, while DG achieves only degree K with the same number. Numerical experiments confirm AF's better error performance. The mapping reveals that Radau polynomials and their zeros connect the two methods, explaining DG's superconvergence.
AI.conclusion: Despite apparent differences, AF and DG are fundamentally the same method for linear problems, with AF being more economical for given accuracy. The connection through Radau polynomials provides natural explanation for DG's superconvergence, revealing AF as the underlying high-order scheme behind DG.

title: Splitting-strategies for arbitrary-order fully mixed finite element discretizations of the Biot equations
summary: We study the fully mixed formulation of the Biot equations, which is characterized by a symmetric coupling between flow and deformation. This structure enables the use of stable mixed finite elements for each subproblem without a strong compatibility condition across the two subphysics. To exploit this flexibility while preserving the conservation structure of both subproblems, we consider fully mixed finite element methods in which the symmetry of the elastic stress tensor is enforced weakly. The resulting mixed formulation exhibits a saddle-point structure whose stability is determined by suitable inf--sup conditions. Inf--sup stability is established for several families of discrete spaces of arbitrary order, leading to optimal a priori error estimates. Iterative splitting strategies following the classical fixed-stress split with additional tuning are specifically investigated for the fully mixed formulation, with proof of convergence and rates depending on the coupling strength. Contrary to previous analyses on coupled problems with a symmetric structure, we theoretically prove the efficacy of negative stabilization, consistent with Schur-complement ideas. Numerical results based on analytical solutions and the classical Mandel problem support the theory.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a fully mixed finite element method for Biot equations with weak symmetry enforcement, establishing stability, optimal error estimates, and analyzing iterative splitting strategies with convergence proofs.
AI.motivation: To develop a flexible mixed finite element method for Biot equations that preserves conservation structure while avoiding strong compatibility conditions between flow and deformation subproblems, enabling use of stable mixed elements for each subphysics.
AI.method: Fully mixed finite element formulation with weak enforcement of elastic stress tensor symmetry, establishing inf-sup stability for arbitrary-order discrete spaces, and analyzing iterative splitting strategies (fixed-stress split with tuning) for the coupled system.
AI.result: Proved inf-sup stability for several families of discrete spaces, optimal a priori error estimates, convergence of iterative splitting strategies with rate dependence on coupling strength, and theoretical proof of negative stabilization efficacy.
AI.conclusion: The fully mixed formulation with weak symmetry enforcement provides a flexible and stable approach for Biot equations, with proven convergence properties for iterative solvers and theoretical justification for stabilization techniques, validated by numerical experiments.

title: Christoffel Adaptive Sampling for Sparse Random Feature Expansions
summary: Random Feature Models (RFMs) have become a powerful tool for approximating multivariate functions and solving partial differential equations efficiently. Sparse Random Feature Expansions (SRFE) improve traditional RFMs by incorporating sparsity, making it particularly effective in data-scarce settings. In this work, we integrate active learning with sparse random feature approximations to improve sampling efficiency. Specifically, we incorporate the Christoffel function to guide an adaptive sampling process, dynamically selecting informative sample points based on their contribution to the function space. This approach optimizes the distribution of sample points by leveraging the Christoffel function associated with an iteratively-chosen basis obtained by the sparse recovery solver. We conduct numerical experiments comparing adaptive and nonadaptive sampling strategies with the SRFE framework and examine their accuracy for various function approximation tasks. Overall, our results demonstrate the advantages of adaptive sampling in maintaining high accuracy while reducing sample complexity for SRFE, highlighting its potential for scientific computing tasks where data is expensive to acquire.

AI.tldr: Active learning with Christoffel function guidance improves sparse random feature models by adaptively selecting informative sample points, reducing data requirements while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Random Feature Models are powerful for function approximation and PDE solving, but traditional methods can be inefficient in data-scarce settings. Sparse Random Feature Expansions help, but sampling efficiency remains a challenge, especially when data acquisition is expensive.
AI.method: Integrate active learning with sparse random feature approximations using the Christoffel function to guide adaptive sampling. Dynamically select informative sample points based on their contribution to the function space, optimizing point distribution using Christoffel function associated with iteratively-chosen basis from sparse recovery solver.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show adaptive sampling maintains high accuracy while reducing sample complexity for Sparse Random Feature Expansions compared to nonadaptive strategies across various function approximation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive sampling with Christoffel function guidance offers significant advantages for SRFE, particularly in scientific computing where data acquisition is expensive, by improving sampling efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Convergence of entropy-stable continuous summation-by-parts discretizations of symmetric hyperbolic conservation laws
summary: The Lax equivalence theorem guarantees convergence of stable and consistent discretizations for linear hyperbolic partial differential equations (PDEs). For nonlinear problems, however, stability and consistency alone do not generally guarantee convergence, even for smooth solutions, and existing convergence results typically rely either on projection-based error decompositions or on linearization arguments that do not directly extend to entropy-stable split-form discretizations. In particular, general convergence results for entropy-stable discretizations of hyperbolic PDEs are currently lacking, despite their widespread use. In this work, we prove convergence under smoothness assumptions on the exact solution and fluxes for entropy-stable split-form discretizations of scalar and symmetric hyperbolic systems with homogeneous flux functions within the continuous summation-by-parts (C-SBP) framework. The scalar inviscid Burgers equation is presented as a canonical example. The analysis is based on a stability-consistency argument that yields a nonlinear error evolution inequality whose solution provides an explicit upper bound on the numerical error. We show that, for sufficiently small mesh spacing, and for degree-$p$ C-SBP discretizations in $d$ spatial dimensions with $p>1+d/2$, this bound remains finite on any finite time interval and tends to zero as the mesh is refined, implying convergence despite the presence of local linear instabilities. The results help clarify the relationship between consistency, entropy stability, nonlinear error growth, and convergence for discretizations of nonlinear hyperbolic problems.

AI.tldr: Proves convergence for entropy-stable split-form discretizations of hyperbolic PDEs using stability-consistency arguments, despite local linear instabilities.
AI.motivation: Lax equivalence theorem doesn't extend to nonlinear hyperbolic PDEs, and existing convergence results don't apply to entropy-stable split-form discretizations, despite their widespread use.
AI.method: Uses stability-consistency argument within continuous summation-by-parts (C-SBP) framework to derive nonlinear error evolution inequality. Analyzes scalar inviscid Burgers equation as canonical example.
AI.result: For degree-p C-SBP discretizations in d spatial dimensions with p>1+d/2, error bound remains finite on finite time intervals and tends to zero with mesh refinement, proving convergence despite local linear instabilities.
AI.conclusion: Establishes convergence theory for entropy-stable discretizations of nonlinear hyperbolic PDEs, clarifying relationship between consistency, entropy stability, nonlinear error growth, and convergence.

title: STAR_Lite: A stellarator designed to experimentally validate non-resonant divertors
summary: The non-resonant divertor (NRD) offers a promising exhaust solution for stellarators, combining topological simplicity with resilience to magnetic field perturbations. To experimentally validate the robustness of non-resonant divertors in a quasi-axisymmetric (QA) configuration, we introduce STAR_Lite, a new stellarator experiment at Hampton University. This paper details the design and analysis of the first STAR_Lite coil configuration, STAR_Lite-A. The two field-period configuration manifests an NRD through X-points with zero rotational transform, at the top and bottom of the device. The divertor legs extruding from the X-points are topologically similar to the poloidal divertors of tokamaks. To expand the experimental range, STAR_Lite-A is optimized for experimental flexibility, producing a wide range of distinct QA configurations by only varying the currents in the modular coils. The NRDs not only persist across these configurations, but numerical strike-line simulations confirm that heat exhaust remains resilient to changes in coil currents, with plasma following the divertor legs and creating a toroidal, discontinuous, strike pattern. We further examine the resilience of the NRD to magnetic perturbations caused by manufacturing errors in the modular coils. We find that quasisymmetry and the existence of X-points is well-preserved under these magnetic field changes, but the rotational transform may vary substantially and displacements of the divertor X-points may lead to one X-point having a dominant effect on edge transport. Overall, our analysis indicates a compact, modular design can likely generate a resilient NRD structure while satisfying the practical constraints of a university-scale experiment.

AI.tldr: STAR_Lite-A is a new stellarator experiment designed to validate non-resonant divertors in quasi-axisymmetric configurations, showing resilient heat exhaust and topological simplicity.
AI.motivation: To experimentally validate the robustness of non-resonant divertors (NRD) in quasi-axisymmetric stellarator configurations, which offer topological simplicity and resilience to magnetic field perturbations compared to traditional divertor solutions.
AI.method: Designed STAR_Lite-A, a two field-period stellarator with modular coils that create NRD through X-points with zero rotational transform. Used numerical strike-line simulations to analyze heat exhaust resilience across various coil current configurations and examined effects of manufacturing errors.
AI.result: NRDs persist across different QA configurations by varying coil currents, with plasma following divertor legs creating toroidal discontinuous strike patterns. Quasisymmetry and X-points are preserved under manufacturing errors, though rotational transform may vary and X-point displacements can affect edge transport dominance.
AI.conclusion: A compact, modular design can generate resilient NRD structures suitable for university-scale experiments, demonstrating the practical feasibility of non-resonant divertors for stellarator exhaust solutions.

title: Generative Replica-Exchange: A Flow-based Framework for Accelerating Replica Exchange Simulations
summary: Replica exchange (REX) is one of the most widely used enhanced sampling methodologies, yet its efficiency is limited by the requirement for a large number of intermediate temperature replicas. Here we present Generative Replica Exchange (GREX), which integrates deep generative models into the REX framework to eliminate this temperature ladder. Drawing inspiration from reservoir replica exchange (res-REX), GREX utilizes trained normalizing flows to generate high-temperature configurations on demand and map them directly to the target distribution using the potential energy as a constraint, without requiring target-temperature training data. This approach reduces production simulations to a single replica at the target temperature while maintaining thermodynamic rigor through Metropolis exchange acceptance. We validate GREX on three benchmark systems of increasing complexity, highlighting its superior efficiency and practical applicability for molecular simulations.

AI.tldr: GREX integrates deep generative models into replica exchange to eliminate the temperature ladder requirement, using normalizing flows to generate high-temperature configurations on demand and mapping them to target distribution via potential energy constraints.
AI.motivation: Traditional replica exchange (REX) requires many intermediate temperature replicas, limiting efficiency. The authors aim to eliminate this temperature ladder requirement while maintaining thermodynamic rigor.
AI.method: GREX integrates deep generative models (normalizing flows) into REX framework. It generates high-temperature configurations on demand using trained normalizing flows and maps them directly to target distribution using potential energy as constraint, without requiring target-temperature training data. Maintains thermodynamic rigor through Metropolis exchange acceptance.
AI.result: Validated on three benchmark systems of increasing complexity, demonstrating superior efficiency and practical applicability for molecular simulations. Reduces production simulations to single replica at target temperature.
AI.conclusion: GREX successfully eliminates the temperature ladder requirement of traditional REX while maintaining thermodynamic rigor, offering superior efficiency for molecular simulations through integration of deep generative models.

title: Nonlinear Kirchhoff-Love shell models derived from the Ciarlet-Geymonat energy: modelling and well-posedness
summary: Starting from a three-dimensional model based on the Ciarlet-Geymonat energy, we derive nonlinear shell models within the classical elasticity theory of compressible isotropic materials. The Neo-Hookean term involving the norm of the deformation gradient leads to an energy depending on the first, the second, and the third fundamental forms of the deformed midsurface. The coefficients appearing in the resulting shell models depend on the classical Lamé coefficients of the three-dimensional material, on the thickness of the shell, and on the mean and Gaussian curvatures of the reference configuration. This shows that the behavior of the shell is influenced not only by the elastic coefficients but also by the initial geometry of the three-dimensional thin body. The purely volumetric Ciarlet-Geymonat contribution of the three-dimensional energy leads to two-dimensional energies depending on the mean and Gaussian curvatures of both configurations, namely the undeformed and the deformed midsurfaces. Since a purely asymptotic derivation may lead to nonlinear terms for which the lower semicontinuity of the resulting functionals is not clear, we combine the asymptotic reduction through the thickness with Simpson's quadrature rule applied to the purely volumetric energy terms, ensuring that the lower semicontinuity is inherited from the three-dimensional model. After deriving the model, we establish the well-posedness of the proposed shell energies. More precisely, we prove coercivity and lower semicontinuity property of the resulting functional and show the existence of minimizers in appropriate Sobolev spaces. A key ingredient in the proofs is a polyconvexity concept in the shell theory, together with some results concerning the weak convergence of terms involving the mean curvature of the deformed midsurface.

AI.tldr: Derivation of nonlinear shell models from 3D elasticity theory showing dependence on initial geometry, with well-posedness proofs using polyconvexity concepts.
AI.motivation: To develop rigorous nonlinear shell models that properly account for both material properties and initial geometry of thin elastic bodies, addressing issues with lower semicontinuity in purely asymptotic derivations.
AI.method: Derive shell models from 3D Ciarlet-Geymonat energy using Neo-Hookean terms, combine asymptotic reduction through thickness with Simpson's quadrature rule for volumetric terms, and establish well-posedness using polyconvexity concepts and weak convergence analysis.
AI.result: Shell models depend on Lamé coefficients, thickness, and mean/Gaussian curvatures of reference configuration; proved coercivity, lower semicontinuity, and existence of minimizers in appropriate Sobolev spaces.
AI.conclusion: Shell behavior depends on both elastic coefficients and initial geometry; developed rigorous mathematical framework with proven well-posedness using polyconvexity and careful handling of volumetric terms.

title: Wavelet-based grid adaptation with consistent treatment of high-order sharp immersed geometries
summary: Wavelet-based grid adaptation methods use multiresolution analysis for error estimation, offering a mathematically rigorous approach to adaptive grid refinement when solving Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). However, applying these methods to PDE discretizations with immersed geometries is challenging, as standard interpolating wavelet transforms lose consistency near non-grid-aligned boundary intersections. To address this, we propose a high-order interpolating wavelet transform adaptation strategy compatible with sharp immersed boundary and interface discretizations. The approach performs consistent high-order wavelet transforms on narrow intervals using a 1D polynomial extrapolation technique. To maintain high order, the technique incorporates boundary values and derivatives, which are evaluated from multivariate interpolating polynomials similar to those used in high order immersed finite difference discretizations. Consequently, the proposed approach maintains the wavelet order on any arbitrary smooth multidimensional domain, including near concave geometry sections. This approach enables grid adaptation in complex domains while robustly bounding the numerical error via a manually set refinement threshold. The algorithm's performance is validated on both static and dynamic problems, including the Navier-Stokes equations with moving boundaries and temporally adapting grid resolutions. The results demonstrate that the proposed method enables effective grid adaptation, establishing a robust, predictable relationship between a user-defined refinement threshold and the overall solution error, even for problems with complex, moving boundaries.

AI.tldr: Proposes a high-order interpolating wavelet transform adaptation strategy for grid refinement in PDEs with immersed boundaries, maintaining wavelet order near complex geometries through polynomial extrapolation techniques.
AI.motivation: Wavelet-based grid adaptation methods offer rigorous error estimation for PDEs, but struggle with immersed geometries where standard wavelet transforms lose consistency near non-grid-aligned boundaries.
AI.method: Develops a high-order interpolating wavelet transform using 1D polynomial extrapolation on narrow intervals, incorporating boundary values and derivatives from multivariate interpolating polynomials similar to high-order immersed finite difference discretizations.
AI.result: The method maintains wavelet order on arbitrary smooth multidimensional domains, enables grid adaptation in complex domains with robust error bounds via refinement threshold, and validates performance on static/dynamic problems including Navier-Stokes with moving boundaries.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables effective grid adaptation with predictable relationship between user-defined refinement threshold and solution error, even for complex moving boundary problems.

title: Quantifying resonant drive in resistive perturbed tokamak equilibria
summary: Resonant drive in tokamaks is routinely quantified using a variety of different metrics that target different aspects of a resonant response to an external perturbation. Two of the most direct metrics, $Δ_{mn}$ and $b_{pen}$, are widely used but their relative behavior was previously uncharacterized. This work examines how these metrics representing the shielding current and penetrated field relate in resistive perturbed tokamak equilibria using asymptotically matched solutions with a resistive MHD inner layer model in GPEC. $b_{pen}$ scales with Lundquist number as $S^{-2/3}$ until saturation at low $S$, and $Δ_{mn}$ remains consistent with its ideal definition but is affected by global kink structure. Both metrics are shown to yield closely similar dominant coupling modes within the same resistive model. However, the resistive physics shifts this dominant mode spectrum to lower poloidal mode numbers $m$ in a low-rotation ITER equilibrium. This alteration is predicted to be observable in experiment in the form of optimal relative phasings of resonant magnetic perturbation coils.

AI.tldr: Comparison of two resonant drive metrics (Δₘₙ and bₚₑₙ) in tokamaks shows they yield similar dominant coupling modes, but resistive physics shifts dominant modes to lower poloidal mode numbers in ITER.
AI.motivation: To understand the relationship between two widely used but previously uncharacterized resonant drive metrics (Δₘₙ and bₚₑₙ) in tokamaks, which measure shielding current and penetrated field respectively.
AI.method: Using asymptotically matched solutions with a resistive MHD inner layer model in GPEC (Generalized Perturbed Equilibrium Code) to analyze resistive perturbed tokamak equilibria.
AI.result: bₚₑₙ scales with Lundquist number as S⁻²/³ until saturation at low S; Δₘₙ remains consistent with ideal definition but affected by global kink structure. Both metrics yield similar dominant coupling modes, but resistive physics shifts dominant modes to lower poloidal mode numbers m in low-rotation ITER equilibrium.
AI.conclusion: The two resonant drive metrics are closely related and predict similar dominant coupling modes, but resistive effects alter the mode spectrum in ITER, which should be observable experimentally through optimal phasing of resonant magnetic perturbation coils.

title: Removing nodal and support-mismatch pathologies in Variational Monte Carlo via blurred sampling
summary: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) is a powerful and fast-growing method for optimizing and evolving parameterized many-body wave functions, especially with modern neural-network quantum states. In practice, however, the stochastic estimators that form the backbone of the method can become unstable or biased due to the presence of nodes, a ubiquitous feature of quantum wave functions. In the continuum, this results in heavy-tailed estimators with potentially divergent variances, while in discrete Hilbert spaces the sampling distribution can miss parts of the support needed to form unbiased estimators. These statistical pathologies lead to unreliable optimization trajectories in stochastic reconfiguration or incorrect variational dynamics in time-dependent Variational Monte Carlo (t-VMC), and severely limit the power of the numerical simulations. We introduce blurred sampling to address these difficulties. The method has a number of rigorous properties that make it well-behaved, effective and efficient. Additionally it is a post-processing approach that can be used without modifying the underlying sampler and incurs only minimal overhead. We demonstrate its effectiveness on several representative examples where standard sampling approaches are known to fail, and apply it to large-scale problems in spin dynamics. This work establishes a broadly applicable framework for robust VMC and t-VMC calculations.

AI.tldr: Blurred sampling method introduced to fix statistical pathologies in Variational Monte Carlo caused by wave function nodes, enabling robust optimization and dynamics calculations.
AI.motivation: Standard VMC suffers from unstable or biased stochastic estimators due to wave function nodes, leading to heavy-tailed estimators with divergent variances in continuum systems and incomplete sampling in discrete spaces. These issues cause unreliable optimization in stochastic reconfiguration and incorrect variational dynamics in time-dependent VMC.
AI.method: Introduces blurred sampling as a post-processing approach that doesn't require modifying the underlying sampler. The method has rigorous properties ensuring well-behaved, effective, and efficient performance with minimal overhead.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness on representative examples where standard sampling approaches fail, and applies it successfully to large-scale spin dynamics problems. The method addresses the statistical pathologies that previously limited VMC simulations.
AI.conclusion: Blurred sampling establishes a broadly applicable framework for robust VMC and t-VMC calculations, overcoming fundamental limitations caused by wave function nodes in quantum Monte Carlo simulations.

title: Global-in-time existence and uniqueness of classical solutions to the unsteady initial-boundary value problem for the four-velocity planar Broadwell model in a rectangular domain
summary: We establish the global-in-time existence and uniqueness of classical solutions to the unsteady initial-boundary value problem for the four-velocity planar Broadwell model in a rectangular domain. The result is obtained for sufficiently small initial and boundary data within the framework of discrete kinetic theory.

AI.tldr: Global existence and uniqueness of classical solutions for the four-velocity planar Broadwell model in rectangular domains with small initial/boundary data.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous mathematical foundations for the unsteady initial-boundary value problem of the four-velocity planar Broadwell model, which is important in discrete kinetic theory for rarefied gas dynamics.
AI.method: Using discrete kinetic theory framework, analyzing the unsteady initial-boundary value problem in rectangular domains with sufficiently small initial and boundary data.
AI.result: Proved global-in-time existence and uniqueness of classical solutions for the four-velocity planar Broadwell model.
AI.conclusion: The four-velocity planar Broadwell model admits well-posed classical solutions globally in time under small data conditions, providing mathematical validation for this discrete kinetic model.

title: Resolving the Blow-Up: A Time-Dilated Numerical Framework for Multiple Firing Events in Mean-Field Neuronal Networks
summary: In large-scale excitatory neuronal networks, rapid synchronization manifests as {multiple firing events (MFEs)}, mathematically characterized by a finite-time blow-up of the neuronal firing rate in the mean-field Fokker-Planck equation. Standard numerical methods struggle to resolve this singularity due to the divergent boundary flux and the instantaneous nature of the population voltage reset. In this work, we propose a robust {multiscale numerical framework based on time dilation}. By transforming the governing equation into a dilated timescale proportional to the firing activity, we desingularize the blow-up, effectively stretching the instantaneous synchronization event into a resolved mesoscopic process. This approach is shown to be physically consistent with the {microscopic cascade mechanism} underlying MFEs and the system's inherent fragility. To implement this numerically, we develop a hybrid scheme that utilizes a {mesh-independent flux criterion} to switch between timescales and a semi-analytical ``moving Gaussian'' method to accurately evolve the post-blowup Dirac mass. Numerical benchmarks demonstrate that our solver not only captures steady states with high accuracy but also efficiently reproduces periodic MFEs, matching Monte Carlo simulations without the severe time-step restrictions associated with particle cascades.

AI.tldr: A multiscale numerical framework using time dilation to resolve finite-time blow-up in neuronal firing rate equations, enabling accurate simulation of rapid synchronization events without severe time-step restrictions.
AI.motivation: Standard numerical methods struggle to resolve rapid synchronization events (multiple firing events) in neuronal networks due to the singularity in firing rate equations and instantaneous voltage reset, requiring a more robust approach.
AI.method: Proposes a time dilation transformation that stretches the governing equation into a dilated timescale proportional to firing activity, desingularizing the blow-up. Uses a hybrid scheme with mesh-independent flux criterion to switch timescales and a semi-analytical "moving Gaussian" method for post-blowup evolution.
AI.result: The solver captures steady states with high accuracy and efficiently reproduces periodic multiple firing events, matching Monte Carlo simulations without the severe time-step restrictions associated with particle cascades.
AI.conclusion: The time dilation framework provides a physically consistent and numerically robust approach to simulate rapid synchronization in neuronal networks, overcoming limitations of standard methods for handling finite-time blow-up phenomena.

title: Quantum Kinetics of Fast-Electron Inelastic Collisions in Partially-Ionized Plasmas
summary: Fast electrons in partially ionized plasmas lose energy through inelastic collisions with bound electrons. While the mean energy loss is well described by stopping-power theory, fluctuations associated with discrete excitation and ionization events produce energy straggling and an additional longitudinal diffusion in momentum space. We incorporate this effect into fast-electron kinetics through a derived Fokker-Planck operator whose coefficients are obtained from ab initio quantum many-body simulations. We demonstrate that neglecting inelastic energy diffusion in partially ionized D-Ar plasmas can underestimate primary runaway-electron generation by several orders of magnitude.

AI.tldr: Inelastic collisions in partially ionized plasmas cause energy diffusion that significantly affects runaway electron generation, with neglect of this effect leading to orders-of-magnitude underestimation.
AI.motivation: While stopping-power theory describes mean energy loss of fast electrons in partially ionized plasmas, fluctuations from discrete excitation/ionization events cause energy straggling and momentum diffusion that are not accounted for in current models.
AI.method: Derived a Fokker-Planck operator with coefficients obtained from ab initio quantum many-body simulations to incorporate inelastic energy diffusion into fast-electron kinetics.
AI.result: Neglecting inelastic energy diffusion in partially ionized D-Ar plasmas can underestimate primary runaway-electron generation by several orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Inelastic energy diffusion from discrete excitation/ionization events is crucial for accurate modeling of runaway electron generation in partially ionized plasmas and must be included in kinetic descriptions.

title: An HHL-Based Quantum-Classical Solver for the Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations with Approximate QST
summary: In computational fluid dynamics (CFD), the numerical integration of the Navier-Stokes equations is frequently constrained by the Poisson equation to determine the pressure. Discretization of this equation often results in the need to solve a system of linear algebraic equations. This step typically represents the primary computational bottleneck. Quantum linear system algorithms such as Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd (HHL) offer the potential for exponential speedups for solving sparse linear systems, such as those that arise from the discretized Poisson equation. In this work, we successfully couple HHL to a discretized formulation of the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations and demonstrate both accurate lid-driven cavity flow simulations as a fully integrated benchmark problem, and accurate flow of the Taylor-Green vortex. To address the readout limitation, we utilize a recent novel quantum state tomography (QST) approach based on Chebyshev polynomials, which enables approximate statevector extraction without full state reconstruction. Together, these results clarify the algorithmic structure required for quantum CFD, explicitly confront the measurement bottleneck, and establish benchmark problems for future quantum fluid simulations. We implement the solver using IBM's Qiskit framework and validate the hybrid quantum-classical simulation against standard classical numerical methods. Our results demonstrate that the hybrid solver successfully captures the global vortex dynamics of the lid-driven cavity problem and the Taylor-Green vortex, offering a robust pathway for integrating quantum subroutines into more practical higher-Reynolds number CFD workflows.

AI.tldr: Quantum-classical hybrid solver using HHL algorithm for Poisson equation in CFD, validated on lid-driven cavity and Taylor-Green vortex problems with quantum state tomography for readout.
AI.motivation: Solving Poisson equation in CFD is computationally expensive bottleneck; quantum algorithms like HHL offer potential exponential speedups for sparse linear systems from discretized Poisson equations.
AI.method: Couple HHL quantum linear system algorithm to discretized incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, use Chebyshev polynomial-based quantum state tomography for approximate statevector extraction, implement with IBM Qiskit framework in hybrid quantum-classical simulation.
AI.result: Hybrid solver successfully captures global vortex dynamics of lid-driven cavity problem and Taylor-Green vortex, validated against classical numerical methods, demonstrates accurate flow simulations.
AI.conclusion: Establishes algorithmic structure for quantum CFD, addresses measurement bottleneck with QST, provides benchmark problems for future quantum fluid simulations, offers pathway for integrating quantum subroutines into higher-Reynolds number CFD workflows.

title: Low-regularity invariant measure for the complex-valued mKdV
summary: In this paper we consider the twice-renormalized, complex-valued modified KdV (mKdV) on the one-dimensional torus introduced by Chapouto. Our main result is the construction of an invariant measure supported at low-regularity. This work complements the work of Kenig et al., which constructed invariant measures supported in higher-regularity spaces for the non-renormalized mKdV. Due to the low-regularity of the support of the measure, we are forced to work in Fourier-Lebesgue spaces. The fact that we consider the complex-valued mKdV makes the problem more complicated than the real-valued case, which was previously considered.

AI.tldr: Construction of invariant measure for complex-valued modified KdV at low regularity using Fourier-Lebesgue spaces
AI.motivation: Previous work by Kenig et al. constructed invariant measures for non-renormalized mKdV in higher-regularity spaces. This paper aims to extend this to low-regularity regimes for the twice-renormalized complex-valued mKdV, which is more challenging than the real-valued case.
AI.method: Working in Fourier-Lebesgue spaces to handle low-regularity support of the measure. Considering the twice-renormalized complex-valued modified KdV equation on one-dimensional torus introduced by Chapouto.
AI.result: Successfully constructed an invariant measure supported at low-regularity for the complex-valued mKdV, complementing previous higher-regularity results.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that invariant measures can be constructed for complex-valued mKdV even at low regularity by working in appropriate function spaces (Fourier-Lebesgue spaces), overcoming challenges not present in the real-valued case.

title: Born-Series-Inspired Residual Metric for Learning-based Preconditioners
summary:
Loss functions for learning-based PDE preconditioners implicitly choose a \emph{metric} in which residuals are matched, yet most approaches still optimize an unpreconditioned Euclidean residual norm. For indefinite operators such as the high-frequency Helmholtz equation, this default metric can make both learning and iterative correction overly sensitive to near-resonant spectral components, while classical preconditioning succeeds precisely by reshaping the residual geometry. We show that the Born Series and shifted-Laplacian left preconditioning are linked by the identity $
I-G_ηV_η= G_ηA = L_η^{-1}A, $
which turns the reference Green operator $G_η$ into a natural Riesz-map residual metric $
R_η= G_η^\ast G_η$
and suggests measuring the physical residual via $
\|r\|_{R_η}=\|G_ηr\|_2. $
Building on this viewpoint, we propose a \emph{Neural Preconditioned Born Series} (NPBS) iteration that replaces the scalar CBS relaxation with a residual-driven neural operator, together with a metric-matched Born-series-inspired loss $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{bs}}^{R_η}$. The framework is architecture-agnostic and supports fast $\mathcal{O}(N\log N)$ evaluation via FFT/DST/DCT. Numerical experiments on heterogeneous Helmholtz problems demonstrate the effectiveness of our method, and its advantage becomes more pronounced as the systems grow more ill-conditioned; we then extend the framework to other PDE classes, including convection--diffusion--reaction equations and linearized Newton systems for nonlinear PDEs, where it also yields substantial iteration reductions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Neural Preconditioned Born Series (NPBS), a learning-based PDE preconditioning framework that uses a Green operator-derived metric to reshape residual geometry, improving convergence for ill-conditioned systems like Helmholtz equations.
AI.motivation: Traditional learning-based PDE preconditioners use unpreconditioned Euclidean residual norms, which are overly sensitive to near-resonant spectral components for indefinite operators like Helmholtz equations. Classical preconditioning succeeds by reshaping residual geometry, suggesting the need for a metric-aware learning approach.
AI.method: The method links Born Series and shifted-Laplacian preconditioning through an identity that turns the reference Green operator into a natural Riesz-map residual metric. It proposes NPBS that replaces scalar CBS relaxation with a residual-driven neural operator and uses a metric-matched Born-series-inspired loss. The framework is architecture-agnostic and supports O(N log N) evaluation via FFT/DST/DCT.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on heterogeneous Helmholtz problems show the method's effectiveness, with advantages becoming more pronounced as systems grow more ill-conditioned. The framework also extends successfully to other PDE classes including convection-diffusion-reaction equations and linearized Newton systems for nonlinear PDEs, yielding substantial iteration reductions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Neural Preconditioned Born Series framework with metric-matched residual geometry provides an effective learning-based preconditioning approach for ill-conditioned PDE systems, outperforming traditional Euclidean metric approaches and extending well to various PDE classes.

title: Evolution of laser-driven magnetic fields from proton tomography
summary: Self-generated magnetic fields are commonly produced in high-power laser-plasma interactions. These fields can inhibit plasma heat-flow which makes them important in inertial fusion and controlled laboratory astrophysics experiments. In this work, we characterize the time evolution of self-generated magnetic fields using multi-view proton tomography at two timings. Tomographic reconstructions of the magnetic field show a clear transition from fields located close to the target at early time to more extended coronal fields at later time. The tomographic inversion and mesh radiography also enable a direct measurement of the magnetic-flux evolution. Comparisons with extended-MHD simulations show only moderate agreement in field structure, but good agreement in magnetic flux. This suggests that the field generation model is largely correct under these conditions, while the magnetic transport model requires additional development to reproduce the observed field structure.

AI.tldr: Researchers used multi-view proton tomography to study self-generated magnetic fields in laser-plasma interactions, showing field evolution from target-proximal to extended coronal structures over time, with good flux agreement but structural differences compared to simulations.
AI.motivation: Self-generated magnetic fields in high-power laser-plasma interactions are important because they inhibit plasma heat-flow, which has significant implications for inertial fusion and laboratory astrophysics experiments. Understanding their evolution is crucial for improving models.
AI.method: Used multi-view proton tomography at two different timings to characterize the time evolution of self-generated magnetic fields. Employed tomographic reconstructions and mesh radiography to directly measure magnetic-flux evolution, and compared results with extended-MHD simulations.
AI.result: Tomographic reconstructions revealed a clear transition: early fields were located close to the target, while later fields became more extended coronal fields. Comparisons with simulations showed moderate agreement in field structure but good agreement in magnetic flux evolution.
AI.conclusion: The field generation model appears largely correct under these conditions, but the magnetic transport model requires additional development to accurately reproduce the observed field structure, highlighting areas for improvement in plasma physics modeling.

title: Multi-Outcome Circuit Optimization for Enhanced Non-Gaussian State Generation
summary: Photonic quantum computing has gained significant interest in recent years due to its potential for scaling to large numbers of qubits. A critical requirement for fault-tolerant quantum computation is the reliable generation of non-Gaussian quantum states, typically achieved using Gaussian operations and photon-number-resolving detectors. However, the probabilistic nature of quantum measurement typically results in low success rates for state preparation. Conventionally, these circuits are optimized to herald a single specific target outcome, thereby disregarding the potential utility of alternative measurement patterns generated by the same physical setup. In this work, we propose and demonstrate a multi-outcome optimization strategy that increases the overall acceptance probability by allowing a single circuit to produce useful quantum states across several measurement patterns. To evaluate this approach, we apply the framework to the generation of Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill core states, Schrodinger cat states, binomial codes, and cubic phase states using both two-mode and three-mode Gaussian circuits. We demonstrate that the success probability can be enhanced through two distinct mechanisms: first, by simultaneously targeting a diverse set of useful resource states, and second, by aggregating degenerate outcomes to maximize the production rate of a single target state.

AI.tldr: Multi-outcome optimization strategy increases success probability for photonic quantum state generation by accepting multiple useful measurement patterns from same circuit.
AI.motivation: Current photonic quantum computing approaches have low success rates for non-Gaussian state generation because they only accept single specific measurement outcomes, wasting alternative useful patterns from same physical setup.
AI.method: Proposed multi-outcome optimization strategy allowing single circuit to produce useful quantum states across several measurement patterns, applied to Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill core states, Schrodinger cat states, binomial codes, and cubic phase states using two-mode and three-mode Gaussian circuits.
AI.result: Success probability enhanced through two mechanisms: simultaneously targeting diverse set of useful resource states, and aggregating degenerate outcomes to maximize production rate of single target state.
AI.conclusion: Multi-outcome optimization significantly improves state preparation efficiency in photonic quantum computing by better utilizing measurement outcomes from existing circuits.

title: Large friction limit of the almost pressureless Euler-Poisson system
summary: The goal of this work is to investigate the almost pressureless Euler-Poisson (EP) system with repulsive force in the large friction limit. The leading order equations in the limit are shown to be the hyperbolic-elliptic Keller-Segel (KS) system of consumption type. Under suitable assumptions on the initial data, we establish the unique global-in-time solutions to both the EP system and the KS system by establishing the global stability in the large friction limit. In particular, no singularity forms in the asymptotic limit. Moreover, the time asymptotic behavior of the one-dimensional KS flow with vacuum is also discussed.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the Euler-Poisson system with repulsive force in large friction limit, showing it converges to Keller-Segel system of consumption type, establishing global solutions and stability without singularity formation.
AI.motivation: To investigate the asymptotic behavior of the almost pressureless Euler-Poisson system with repulsive force in the large friction limit, connecting it to the Keller-Segel system and understanding the global dynamics without singularity formation.
AI.method: Analyze the Euler-Poisson system with repulsive force, perform large friction limit analysis to derive leading order equations (Keller-Segel system), establish global-in-time solutions for both systems through stability analysis in the limit.
AI.result: Proves unique global-in-time solutions exist for both Euler-Poisson and Keller-Segel systems, establishes global stability in the large friction limit, shows no singularity forms in asymptotic limit, and discusses time asymptotic behavior of 1D KS flow with vacuum.
AI.conclusion: The Euler-Poisson system with repulsive force converges to Keller-Segel system of consumption type in large friction limit, with global stability and no singularity formation, providing mathematical foundation for understanding these fluid-kinetic systems.

title: Reconstructions of Single Pixel X-Ray Transforms with Applications in Nuclear-Disarmament Verification
summary: In nuclear arms control and disarmament processes, it is crucial to determine whether an object is a nuclear weapon or not without revealing sensitive information about it. At the MIT: Laboratory for Nuclear Security and Policy, such a nuclear verification method was developed, showcasing a transmission-based approach [1]. This method's essential part rests on a mathematical operation, the Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform: a cone of X-rays transmits an object and the remaining intensity is measured with a single-pixel detector. This transformation and the recovery of objects from dimensionless single-pixel measurements more generally has only been analyzed to a limited extent. In this work, we investigate some of the Single Pixel X-Ray Transform's mathematical properties. More specifically, we show that the Single Pixel X-ray transform is non-linear, continuous, Fréchet-differentiable and convex. We also introduce a method of reconstructing an object based only on a finite number of dimensionless, noisy Single Pixel X-Ray Transform measurement values. This method is based on Douglas-Rachford splitting and uses total variation denoising. We present an implementation for this method, focusing on rotational symmetric objects, as they allow the use of a one-dimensional direct total variation denoising algorithm [2].

AI.tldr: A mathematical analysis of the Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform for nuclear verification, showing its properties and developing a reconstruction method using Douglas-Rachford splitting with total variation denoising.
AI.motivation: Nuclear arms control requires verifying objects as nuclear weapons without revealing sensitive information. The Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform was developed for this purpose but lacks comprehensive mathematical analysis and reconstruction methods from limited measurements.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of the Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform properties (non-linearity, continuity, Fréchet-differentiability, convexity). Development of reconstruction method using Douglas-Rachford splitting with total variation denoising, implemented for rotationally symmetric objects using 1D direct TV denoising.
AI.result: The Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform is proven to be non-linear, continuous, Fréchet-differentiable and convex. A reconstruction method is developed that works with finite, dimensionless, noisy measurements, with implementation for rotationally symmetric objects.
AI.conclusion: The mathematical foundations of the Single-Pixel X-Ray Transform are established, enabling practical reconstruction of objects from limited measurements for nuclear verification applications while protecting sensitive information.

title: Generation of Whistler Waves by Reflected Electrons and Their Self-Confinement at Quasi-Perpendicular Shocks
summary: We investigate the mechanism of whistler-mode wave generation by shock-reflected electrons at quasi-perpendicular collisionless shocks. By employing Liouville mapping to construct the electron velocity distribution function in the shock and performing linear instability analysis, we explore whistler wave generation by the mirror-reflected electrons near the upstream edge of the shock transition layer. We find that the reflected electrons can excite two distinct instabilities with different propagation directions when both the upstream electron beta $β_e$ and Alfven Mach number in the de Hoffmann-Teller frame $M_A/\cosθ_{bn}$ are sufficiently large, where $M_A$ is \Alfven Mach number and $θ_{bn}$ is the angle between the upstream magnetic field and the shock normal. In the parameter regime of Earth's bow shock, the instability threshold condition is roughly given by $M_A/\cosθ_{bn}\gtrsim50$. Since such shocks are super-critical with respect to the whistler critical Mach number, the generated waves cannot propagate upstream and will accumulate in the transition layer. Furthermore, we find that the pitch-angle scattering by the generated waves may trigger secondary instabilities on the same branch. We suggest that the sequence of instabilities likely happening within the shock transition layer can efficiently scatter the reflected electrons over a broad range of pitch angles. Consequently, the reflected electrons may be confined within the shock by the waves generated by themselves. The self-confinement provides the necessary ingredient of stochastic shock drift acceleration, which then offers a plausible mechanism for the electron injection into diffusive shock acceleration.

AI.tldr: Whistler-mode waves generated by shock-reflected electrons at quasi-perpendicular shocks can self-confine electrons, enabling stochastic shock drift acceleration and electron injection into diffusive shock acceleration.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanism of whistler-mode wave generation by shock-reflected electrons at quasi-perpendicular collisionless shocks and how this affects electron acceleration processes.
AI.method: Used Liouville mapping to construct electron velocity distribution functions in shocks, performed linear instability analysis to explore whistler wave generation by mirror-reflected electrons near the upstream edge of shock transition layers.
AI.result: Reflected electrons can excite two distinct instabilities with different propagation directions when both upstream electron beta and Alfven Mach number are sufficiently large. For Earth's bow shock, threshold is roughly MA/cosθbn ≳ 50. Generated waves cannot propagate upstream in super-critical shocks, accumulating in transition layer and potentially triggering secondary instabilities.
AI.conclusion: The sequence of instabilities within shock transition layers can efficiently scatter reflected electrons over broad pitch angles, confining them within the shock via self-generated waves. This self-confinement enables stochastic shock drift acceleration, providing a plausible mechanism for electron injection into diffusive shock acceleration.

title: On divergence operators: Free space and vanishing charges
summary: We use localized topologies to prove existence and optimal regularity results for the divergence equation $\mathrm{div} (v) = F$ in critical cases $v \in L_1(Ω;\mathbb{R}^m)$ or $v \in C_0(Ω;\mathbb{R}^m)$, i.e. we characterize those $F$ for which a solution $v$ exists whose norm is bounded by an appropriate norm of $F$. We assume $Ω$ satisfies a Poincaré inequality or an extension property. We apply the general theory to give examples of admissible $F$ in each case.

AI.tldr: The paper studies existence and optimal regularity for divergence equations in critical function spaces (L₁ and C₀), characterizing which F admit solutions with appropriate norm bounds.
AI.motivation: To understand which right-hand sides F allow for solutions to the divergence equation in critical function spaces, which is important for applications in PDE theory and fluid dynamics where optimal regularity results are needed.
AI.method: Uses localized topologies and assumes domain Ω satisfies either a Poincaré inequality or an extension property. Applies general theory to construct examples of admissible F in each critical case.
AI.result: Characterizes precisely those F for which solutions v ∈ L₁(Ω;ℝᵐ) or v ∈ C₀(Ω;ℝᵐ) exist with appropriate norm bounds, providing existence and optimal regularity results in these critical cases.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a comprehensive theory for divergence equations in critical function spaces, with applications to constructing admissible right-hand sides F under appropriate domain conditions.

title: Analysis of Convergence for the IPA-AC Method
summary: The Improved Partial Area-Analytical Calculation (IPA-AC) method represents a leading meshfree discretization strategy for peridynamic models, distinguished by its rigorous geometric treatment of boundary intersections via dual corrections of integration weights and quadrature points. Despite its empirical success in suppressing boundary-induced geometric errors, a systematic theoretical characterization of its convergence behaviors under distinct scaling limits has remained elusive. This work establishes a unified convergence framework for the IPA-AC method applied to both scalar and tensor kernels. By leveraging the Lax Equivalence Theorem, we explicitly derive error estimates that reveal the method's performance across three critical limiting regimes. The theoretical analysis, substantiated by numerical validation, demonstrates that: (1) for a fixed horizon $δ$, the method achieves robust second-order convergence $\mathcal{O}(h ^{2})$ with respect to the mesh size $h$; (2) for a fixed mesh, the discretization error scales as $\mathcal{O}(δ^{-2})$, indicating a sensitivity to the nonlocal length scale; and (3) the method does not satisfy the Asymptotic Compatibility (AC) condition. These findings clarify that while the IPA-AC method offers superior accuracy for simulating fixed nonlocal models, it requires a sufficiently large horizon-to-mesh ratio to mitigate intrinsic discretization errors when approximating the local limit.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes convergence theory for the IPA-AC meshfree method in peridynamics, showing it achieves O(h²) convergence for fixed horizon, O(δ⁻²) scaling for fixed mesh, but fails to satisfy Asymptotic Compatibility.
AI.motivation: Despite empirical success of the IPA-AC method in suppressing boundary-induced geometric errors in peridynamic models, there has been no systematic theoretical characterization of its convergence behaviors under different scaling limits.
AI.method: The authors establish a unified convergence framework using the Lax Equivalence Theorem to derive error estimates for the IPA-AC method applied to both scalar and tensor kernels, analyzing three critical limiting regimes.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows: (1) O(h²) convergence for fixed horizon δ, (2) O(δ⁻²) error scaling for fixed mesh, and (3) failure to satisfy Asymptotic Compatibility condition. Numerical validation supports these findings.
AI.conclusion: While IPA-AC offers superior accuracy for fixed nonlocal models, it requires large horizon-to-mesh ratios to mitigate discretization errors when approximating the local limit, and does not achieve Asymptotic Compatibility.

title: A finite-difference model for intense light interactions with dielectrics in the ultrafast ionization regime
summary: We present a computationally efficient model that describes the interaction of intense, ultrashort infrared laser pulses with transparent materials in the strong ionization regime. The model is augmented with a detailed self-consistent description of the local response due to ionization and collisional plasma dynamics. It incorporates the direct solution of Maxwell's equations without approximations and rigorous boundary conditions for the input pulse, allowing us to study the ultrafast formation of over-critical nanoscaled plasmas in dielectric materials under the influence of intense tightly focused laser pulses. We perform a scan of the parameter space, find unexpected optima regimes for different experientially relevant parameters, and explain these maxima based on spatiotemporal dynamics.

AI.tldr: Efficient computational model for intense ultrashort laser interactions with transparent materials, featuring self-consistent ionization/plasma dynamics and full Maxwell solutions to study ultrafast nanoplasma formation.
AI.motivation: To understand and model the complex interactions between intense, ultrashort infrared laser pulses and transparent materials in the strong ionization regime, particularly focusing on ultrafast formation of over-critical nanoscaled plasmas.
AI.method: Developed a computationally efficient model with detailed self-consistent description of local response from ionization and collisional plasma dynamics. Incorporates direct solution of Maxwell's equations without approximations and rigorous boundary conditions for input pulses. Used to study tightly focused laser pulses on dielectric materials.
AI.result: Performed parameter space scans and discovered unexpected optimal regimes for different experimentally relevant parameters. Explained these maxima based on spatiotemporal dynamics of the laser-material interaction.
AI.conclusion: The model successfully describes complex laser-material interactions in strong ionization regimes, revealing optimal parameter regimes and providing insights into spatiotemporal dynamics of ultrafast nanoplasma formation in transparent materials.

title: Acoustic radiation of thermodiffusively unstable turbulent lean premixed hydrogen-air flames
summary: The impact of thermodiffusive effects on combustion noise in turbulent premixed slot jet flames is investigated using Direct Numerical Simulations. Two thermodiffusively unstable lean hydrogen-air flames are compared with a thermodiffusively stable stoichiometric methane-air flame with comparable laminar properties and same turbulence intensity. The hydrogen cases differ in bulk velocity, chosen to match either the turbulent flame brush length or the bulk velocity of the methane case. Thermodiffusive effects are found to strongly alter both the heat release rate fluctuations, which dominate the far-field acoustic radiation, and the flame surface dynamics. A theoretical framework extending the classical flamelet theory to thermodiffusively unstable flames is proposed and validated, relating the flame-generated sound to the time derivative of the flame surface area and to the stretch factor $I_0$. The analysis identifies flame stretch as a key mechanism promoting noise radiation in thermodiffusively unstable flames. Spectral analyses further show that hydrogen flames exhibit stronger low-frequency heat release rate fluctuations and reduced high-frequency content relative to the methane flame. This is shown to be related to the coupled action of turbulence and thermodiffusive instabilities, which enhance large-scale flame motions while attenuating small-scale flame annihilation events. Consequently, hydrogen flames radiate more strongly at low frequencies, near the acoustic peak, and exhibit a steeper high-frequency spectral roll-off. Finally, Spectral Proper Orthogonal Decomposition reveals that hydrogen non-equidiffusion intensifies shear layer instabilities between combustion products and ambient air. These results indicate that thermodiffusive effects influence both direct and indirect combustion noise generation mechanisms in hydrogen flames.

AI.tldr: Thermodiffusive effects in turbulent premixed flames significantly alter combustion noise generation, with hydrogen flames showing stronger low-frequency noise and different spectral characteristics compared to methane flames due to flame stretch and instability mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To understand how thermodiffusive effects influence combustion noise generation in turbulent premixed flames, particularly comparing thermodiffusively unstable hydrogen-air flames with stable methane-air flames.
AI.method: Direct Numerical Simulations of slot jet flames comparing two lean hydrogen-air flames (thermodiffusively unstable) with a stoichiometric methane-air flame (thermodiffusively stable). Developed theoretical framework extending classical flamelet theory to account for thermodiffusive instabilities, and used Spectral Proper Orthogonal Decomposition for analysis.
AI.result: Hydrogen flames exhibit stronger low-frequency heat release rate fluctuations and reduced high-frequency content compared to methane flames. Thermodiffusive effects enhance large-scale flame motions while attenuating small-scale flame annihilation events. Flame stretch is identified as key mechanism promoting noise radiation in thermodiffusively unstable flames.
AI.conclusion: Thermodiffusive effects significantly influence both direct and indirect combustion noise generation mechanisms in hydrogen flames, altering spectral characteristics and intensifying shear layer instabilities between combustion products and ambient air.

title: Neumann problem with a discontinuous nonlinearity
summary: This study is devoted to proving the existence of weak solutions for a nonlinear elliptic problem with Neumann-type boundary data. The problem is driven by a discontinuous power nonlinearity and a nonsmooth prescribed data. Additionally, we aim to derive an estimate that proves the well-posedness of the problem. This estimate serves as an evidence for the uniqueness of the existing solution when the boundary term is ``smooth".

AI.tldr: Existence of weak solutions for nonlinear elliptic problem with Neumann boundary conditions, discontinuous power nonlinearity, and nonsmooth data, with well-posedness estimate.
AI.motivation: To establish existence results for nonlinear elliptic problems with challenging features: Neumann-type boundary conditions, discontinuous power nonlinearities, and nonsmooth prescribed data, which are common in applications but mathematically difficult to handle.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis approach using functional analysis and PDE theory to prove existence of weak solutions. The method likely involves variational techniques, fixed point arguments, or monotone operator theory to handle the discontinuous nonlinearity and nonsmooth data.
AI.result: Proves existence of weak solutions for the nonlinear elliptic problem. Derives a well-posedness estimate that serves as evidence for uniqueness when the boundary term is smooth.
AI.conclusion: The study successfully establishes existence of solutions for challenging nonlinear elliptic problems with Neumann boundary conditions and discontinuous nonlinearities, providing mathematical tools for analyzing such problems with practical applications.

title: Model Order Reduction of Cerebrovascular Hemodynamics Using POD_Galerkin and Reservoir Computing_based Approach
summary: We investigate model order reduction (MOR) strategies for simulating unsteady hemodynamics within cerebrovascular systems, contrasting a physics-based intrusive approach with a data-driven non-intrusive framework. High-fidelity 3D Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) snapshots of an idealised basilar artery bifurcation are first compressed into a low-dimensional latent space using Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD). We evaluate the performance of a POD-Galerkin (POD-G) model, which projects the Navier-Stokes equations onto the reduced basis, against a POD-Reservoir Computing (POD-RC) model that learns the temporal evolution of coefficients through a recurrent architecture. A multi-harmonic and multi-amplitude training signal is introduced to improve training efficiency. Both methodologies achieve computational speed-ups on the order of 10^2 to 10^3 compared to full-order simulations, demonstrating their potential as efficient and accurate surrogates for predicting flow quantities such as wall shear stress.

AI.tldr: The paper compares two model order reduction approaches (intrusive POD-Galerkin vs. non-intrusive POD-Reservoir Computing) for simulating unsteady hemodynamics in cerebrovascular systems, achieving 100-1000x speed-up with good accuracy.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient computational methods for simulating unsteady hemodynamics in cerebrovascular systems, as traditional 3D CFD simulations are computationally expensive for clinical applications.
AI.method: Two MOR approaches: 1) Intrusive POD-Galerkin (POD-G) that projects Navier-Stokes equations onto reduced basis, and 2) Non-intrusive POD-Reservoir Computing (POD-RC) that learns temporal evolution using recurrent architecture. Both use Proper Orthogonal Decomposition for dimensionality reduction and multi-harmonic training signals.
AI.result: Both methods achieved computational speed-ups of 10^2 to 10^3 compared to full-order simulations while maintaining accuracy for predicting flow quantities like wall shear stress in an idealized basilar artery bifurcation.
AI.conclusion: Both intrusive and non-intrusive MOR approaches provide efficient and accurate surrogates for hemodynamic simulations, with potential for clinical applications requiring rapid flow predictions.

title: Utility-scale quantum computational chemistry
summary: Chemistry and materials science are widely regarded as potential killer application fields for quantum hardware. While the dream of unlocking unprecedented simulation capabilities remains compelling, quantum algorithm development must adapt to the evolving constraints of the emerging quantum hardware in order to accomplish any advantage for the computational chemistry practice. At the same time, the continuous advancement of classical wavefunction-theory methods narrows the window for a broad quantum advantage. Here, we explore potential benefits of quantum computation from the broader perspective of utility-scale applications. We argue that quantum algorithms need not only enable accurate calculations for a few challenging, that is strongly correlated, molecular structures, that might be hard to describe with traditional methods. Instead, they must also support the practical integration of quantum-accelerated computations into high-throughput pipelines for routine calculations on arbitrary molecules, ultimately delivering a tangible value to society.

AI.tldr: Quantum computing for chemistry must adapt to hardware constraints and integrate into practical workflows to deliver real-world value, not just solve niche problems.
AI.motivation: While quantum computing holds promise for chemistry and materials science, current quantum algorithm development must adapt to emerging hardware limitations and compete with advancing classical methods to achieve practical advantage.
AI.method: The paper takes a utility-scale perspective, arguing that quantum algorithms should support integration into high-throughput pipelines for routine calculations on arbitrary molecules rather than focusing only on strongly correlated systems.
AI.result: The analysis suggests that quantum advantage in chemistry requires not just accurate calculations for challenging molecular structures, but practical workflow integration that delivers tangible societal value.
AI.conclusion: Quantum computing for chemistry must evolve from solving niche problems to enabling utility-scale applications integrated into routine computational workflows to achieve meaningful impact.

title: Spreading of pathological proteins through brain networks: a case study for Alzheimers disease
summary: Mathematical modeling offers a valuable approach to understanding Alzheimers disease (AD) given its complexity, unknown causes, and lack of effective treatments. Models, once validated, offer a powerful tool to test medical hypotheses that are otherwise difficult to verify directly. Our focus here is on elucidating the spread of misfolded tau protein, a critical hallmark of AD alongside Abeta protein, taking also into account the synergistic interaction between the two proteins. We consider distinct modelling choices, all employing network frameworks for protein evolution, differentiated by their network architecture and diffusion operators. By carefully comparing these models against clinical tau concentration data, gathered through advanced multimodal analysis techniques, we show that certain models replicate better the proteins dynamics. This investigation underscores a crucial insight: in modeling complex pathologies, the precision with which the mathematical framework is chosen is crucial, especially when validation against clinical data is considered decisive.

AI.tldr: Mathematical modeling of Alzheimer's disease focusing on tau protein spread and Abeta-tau interactions, comparing different network-based models against clinical data to identify best-performing frameworks.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease is complex with unknown causes and no effective treatments. Mathematical modeling provides a valuable approach to understand AD and test medical hypotheses that are difficult to verify directly, particularly regarding the spread of misfolded tau protein and its interaction with Abeta.
AI.method: Employed network frameworks for protein evolution with distinct modeling choices differentiated by network architecture and diffusion operators. Compared these models against clinical tau concentration data gathered through advanced multimodal analysis techniques.
AI.result: Certain models replicate tau protein dynamics better than others when validated against clinical data, demonstrating that model selection precision is crucial for accurately representing complex pathologies.
AI.conclusion: The choice of mathematical framework is critical when modeling complex diseases like Alzheimer's, especially when validation against clinical data is considered decisive for model reliability and predictive power.

title: A divided difference identity for a class of multiple integrals
summary: We derive an identity that relates a class of multiple integrals involving Vandermonde polynomials to divided differences. Alternatively the identity can be viewed as an integral formula for divided differences. As part of the derivation we show that both sums of pure partial derivatives and mixed partial derivatives of Vandermonde polynomials are zero, which might be of independent interest.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an identity connecting multiple integrals of Vandermonde polynomials to divided differences, which can also be interpreted as an integral formula for divided differences.
AI.motivation: To establish a mathematical relationship between two important concepts in analysis and combinatorics: multiple integrals involving Vandermonde polynomials and divided differences, which are fundamental in interpolation theory and numerical analysis.
AI.method: The authors derive an identity through mathematical analysis, proving that sums of both pure and mixed partial derivatives of Vandermonde polynomials vanish, which serves as a key intermediate result in establishing the main identity.
AI.result: The main result is an identity that relates a class of multiple integrals with Vandermonde polynomials to divided differences, providing an alternative integral representation for divided differences.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a novel connection between integration theory involving Vandermonde polynomials and divided difference calculus, with the additional finding about vanishing sums of partial derivatives of Vandermonde polynomials potentially having independent mathematical significance.

title: A stable and fast method for solving multibody scattering problems via the method of fundamental solutions
summary: The paper describes a numerical method for solving acoustic multibody scattering problems in two and three dimensions. The idea is to compute a highly accurate approximation to the scattering operator for each body through a local computation, and then use these scattering matrices to form a global linear system. The resulting coefficient matrix is relatively well-conditioned, even for problems involving a very large number of scatterers. The linear system is amenable to iterative solvers, and can readily be accelerated via fast algorithms for the matrix-vector multiplication such as the fast multipole method. The key point of the work is that the local scattering matrices can be constructed using potentially ill-conditioned techniques such as the method of fundamental solutions (MFS), while still maintaining scalability and numerical stability of the global solver. The resulting algorithm is simple, as the MFS is far simpler to implement than alternative techniques based on discretizing boundary integral equations using Nyström or Galerkin.

AI.tldr: A numerical method for acoustic multibody scattering problems that computes local scattering matrices for each body and combines them into a global system, maintaining scalability and stability even when using potentially ill-conditioned local methods like MFS.
AI.motivation: To develop a scalable and numerically stable method for solving acoustic multibody scattering problems that can handle large numbers of scatterers while being simpler to implement than traditional boundary integral equation methods.
AI.method: Compute highly accurate local scattering matrices for each body using potentially ill-conditioned techniques like the Method of Fundamental Solutions (MFS), then combine these into a global linear system that remains well-conditioned and amenable to iterative solvers with fast matrix-vector multiplication acceleration.
AI.result: The resulting global coefficient matrix is relatively well-conditioned even for problems with very large numbers of scatterers, and the linear system can be efficiently solved using iterative methods accelerated by fast algorithms like the Fast Multipole Method.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully decouples local computations from global solution, allowing use of simple but potentially ill-conditioned local methods like MFS while maintaining overall scalability and numerical stability for large-scale acoustic multibody scattering problems.

title: Existence Theory for a Cross-Diffusion System with Independent Drifts: Mixing Dynamics
summary: We consider a cross-diffusion system for which the diffusion of each species is governed solely by the aggregate density through a pressure law of logarithmic or fast diffusion type. The model is set over a one dimensional bounded interval, equipped with no-flux boundary conditions, and accommodates for the presence of potential drifts which are allowed to differ across each species. We establish the global existence of solutions without having to assume the total mixing of solutions. As a consequence, we give a full resolution of the PDE systems recently studied by the authors and by Elbar--Santambrogio, by allowing a general class of initial data with finite bounded variation, with no further structural assumptions on their supports.

AI.tldr: Global existence of solutions for cross-diffusion systems with pressure-dependent diffusion and species-specific potential drifts in 1D, without requiring total mixing assumptions.
AI.motivation: To resolve PDE systems studied in recent works by allowing general initial data with finite bounded variation, removing structural assumptions on supports and total mixing requirements.
AI.method: Analysis of cross-diffusion system with logarithmic/fast diffusion pressure law, set on 1D bounded interval with no-flux boundary conditions, accommodating species-specific potential drifts.
