AI.result: Established global existence of solutions without assuming total mixing, providing full resolution for previously studied PDE systems with general initial data of finite bounded variation.
AI.conclusion: The work extends previous results by removing restrictive assumptions on initial data, allowing more general applications of cross-diffusion models with pressure-dependent diffusion.

title: A Stabilized Mortar Method for Discontinuities in Geological Media with Non-Conforming Grids
summary: Accurate numerical simulation of fault and fracture mechanics is critical for the performance and safety assessment of many subsurface systems. The discretized representation of discontinuity surfaces and the robust simulation of their frictional contact behavior still represent major challenges. In this work, we use the mortar method to enforce the contact constraints and allow for non-conformity around the discontinuity surface, with a set of Lagrange multipliers playing the role of interface tractions. The formulation combines piecewise linear displacements with piecewise constant multipliers defined on one side of the fault interface (the non-mortar side). This choice for the Lagrange multipliers has a number of advantages from practical and computational viewpoints, but violates the inf-sup stability constraint. In order to stabilize the proposed formulation, we develop a traction-jump stabilization term to be added to the constraint equations. We use a macro-element analysis to derive an algorithmic strategy that automatically evaluates the proper scaling of the stabilization, without requiring any additional user-selected parameter. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed formulation not only restores the inf-sup stability condition, but also recovers stable traction profiles in the presence of finer non-mortar sides, where other inf-sup-stable formulations fail. The proposed method is finally used to simulate non-linear contact conditions in non-conforming corner-point grids typically used in industrial geological applications.

AI.tldr: A mortar method with piecewise constant Lagrange multipliers for fault/fracture contact simulation, stabilized via traction-jump stabilization with automatic scaling, enabling stable non-conforming grids for subsurface applications.
AI.motivation: Accurate simulation of fault and fracture mechanics is critical for subsurface system performance and safety assessment, but discretized representation of discontinuity surfaces and robust simulation of frictional contact behavior remain major challenges.
AI.method: Uses mortar method with piecewise linear displacements and piecewise constant Lagrange multipliers (on non-mortar side) for contact constraints. Develops traction-jump stabilization term to address inf-sup stability violation. Employs macro-element analysis for automatic stabilization scaling without user parameters.
AI.result: The formulation restores inf-sup stability and recovers stable traction profiles even with finer non-mortar sides, where other inf-sup-stable formulations fail. Successfully simulates non-linear contact conditions in non-conforming corner-point grids used in industrial geological applications.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stabilized mortar method with piecewise constant multipliers and automatic stabilization scaling provides a robust, parameter-free approach for simulating fault/fracture contact mechanics in non-conforming geological grids, addressing key challenges in subsurface system modeling.

title: On single-frequency asymptotics for the Maxwell-Bloch equations: mixed states
comment: 15 pages. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2603.17888
summary: We consider damped driven Maxwell-Bloch equations which are finite-dimensional approximation of the damped driven Maxwell-Schrödinger equations. The equations describe a single-mode Maxwell field coupled to a two-level molecule. Our main result is the construction of solutions with single-frequency asymptotics of the Maxwell field in the case of quasiperiodic pumping. The asymptotics hold for solutions with harmonic initial values which are stationary states of averaged equations in the interaction picture. We calculate all harmonic states and analyse their stability. The calculations rely on the Bloch-Feynman gyroscopic representation of von Neumann equation for the density matrix. The asymptotics follow by application of the averaging theory of the Bogolyubov type.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes damped driven Maxwell-Bloch equations (finite-dimensional approximation of Maxwell-Schrödinger equations) describing a single-mode Maxwell field coupled to a two-level molecule, constructing solutions with single-frequency asymptotics for quasiperiodic pumping.
AI.motivation: To understand the dynamics of quantum optical systems where a single-mode Maxwell field interacts with a two-level molecule, particularly focusing on establishing solutions with well-defined frequency asymptotics under quasiperiodic driving conditions.
AI.method: Uses damped driven Maxwell-Bloch equations as finite-dimensional approximation; employs Bloch-Feynman gyroscopic representation of von Neumann equation for density matrix; applies averaging theory of Bogolyubov type to derive asymptotics; analyzes harmonic states and their stability.
AI.result: Successfully constructs solutions with single-frequency asymptotics of the Maxwell field for quasiperiodic pumping; calculates all harmonic states and analyzes their stability; establishes asymptotics for solutions with harmonic initial values that are stationary states of averaged equations in the interaction picture.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework for analyzing quantum optical systems with quasiperiodic driving, demonstrating that single-frequency asymptotic solutions exist and can be systematically derived using averaging methods and gyroscopic representations.

title: Non-asymptotic uniform in time error bounds for new and old numerical schemes for SPDEs
summary: We study numerical schemes for Stochastic Partial Differential Equations (SPDEs). We introduce a general method of proof of non-asymptotic uniform in time error bounds on numerical integrators for SPDEs, ensuring the schemes capture both the transient and the long term dynamics faithfully. We then consider SPDEs with non-globally Lipshitz nonlinearities, which include for example the stochastic Allen-Cahn equation and some stochastic advection-diffusion equations. For the case of Allen-Cahn type SPDEs we show that the classic semi-implicit Euler time-discretization can exhibit finite time blow up. This motivates analysing other schemes which do not suffer from this blow-up problem. We consider three numerical schemes for SPDEs with non globally Lipshitz nonlinearity: a fully implicit scheme and two tamed schemes. For these schemes we prove non-asymptotic uniform in time error bounds by leveraging our general criterion, and provide numerical comparisons. While the main emphasis in this paper is on the properties of the time-discretization, the schemes we consider are full space-time discretization of the SPDE.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a general method for proving non-asymptotic uniform-in-time error bounds for numerical schemes of SPDEs, focusing on schemes that can handle non-globally Lipschitz nonlinearities without finite-time blow-up.
AI.motivation: Standard numerical schemes for SPDEs with non-globally Lipschitz nonlinearities (like stochastic Allen-Cahn equation) can exhibit finite-time blow-up, motivating the need for robust schemes that capture both transient and long-term dynamics faithfully.
AI.method: Develops a general proof method for uniform-in-time error bounds, then analyzes three specific schemes for non-globally Lipschitz SPDEs: a fully implicit scheme and two tamed schemes, considering full space-time discretization.
AI.result: Shows that classic semi-implicit Euler discretization can blow up for Allen-Cahn type SPDEs, while the proposed implicit and tamed schemes avoid this problem and achieve non-asymptotic uniform-in-time error bounds.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a general framework for analyzing numerical schemes for SPDEs with challenging nonlinearities, demonstrating that carefully designed implicit and tamed schemes can overcome blow-up issues while maintaining accurate long-term behavior.

title: Physics-grounded Mechanism Design for Spectrum Sharing between Passive and Active Users
summary: We propose a physics-grounded mechanism design for dynamic spectrum sharing that bridges the gap between radiometric retrieval constraints and economic incentives. We formulate the active and passive users coexistence problem as a Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) auctions mechanism, where the radiometer dynamically procures ``quiet'' time-frequency tiles from active users based on the marginal reduction in retrieval error variance. This approach ensures allocative efficiency and dominant-strategy incentive compatibility (DSIC). To overcome the computational intractability of exact VCG on large grids, we derive an approximation algorithm by using the monotone submodularity induced by the radiometer equation. AMSR-2-based simulations show that the approach avoids high-cost tiles by aggregating low-cost spectrum across time and frequency. In an interference-trap case study, the proposed framework reduces procurement costs by about 60% over a fixed-band baseline while satisfying accuracy targets.

AI.tldr: Physics-grounded mechanism design for dynamic spectrum sharing that connects radiometric retrieval constraints with economic incentives using VCG auctions.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between radiometric retrieval constraints and economic incentives in dynamic spectrum sharing, addressing the coexistence problem between active and passive users.
AI.method: Formulate the coexistence problem as a Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) auction mechanism where radiometers procure "quiet" time-frequency tiles from active users based on marginal reduction in retrieval error variance. Use approximation algorithm leveraging monotone submodularity from radiometer equation to overcome computational complexity.
AI.result: AMSR-2-based simulations show the approach avoids high-cost tiles by aggregating low-cost spectrum across time and frequency. In interference-trap case study, reduces procurement costs by about 60% over fixed-band baseline while meeting accuracy targets.
AI.conclusion: Proposed framework successfully integrates physics constraints with economic mechanisms for efficient spectrum sharing, achieving significant cost savings while maintaining radiometric accuracy through incentive-compatible auction design.

title: On Affordable High-Order Entropy-Conservative/Stable and Well-Balanced Methods for Nonconservative Hyperbolic Systems
summary: Many entropy-conservative and entropy-stable (summarized as entropy-preserving) methods for hyperbolic conservation laws rely on Tadmor's theory for two-point entropy-preserving numerical fluxes and its higher-order extension via flux differencing using summation-by-parts (SBP) operators, e.g., in discontinuous Galerkin spectral element methods (DGSEMs). The underlying two-point formulations have been extended to nonconservative systems using fluctuations by Castro et al. (2013, doi:10.1137/110845379) with follow-up generalizations to SBP methods. We propose specific forms of entropy-preserving fluctuations for nonconservative hyperbolic systems that are simple to interpret and allow an algorithmic construction of entropy-preserving methods. We analyze necessary and sufficient conditions, and obtain a full characterization of entropy-preserving three-point methods within the finite volume framework. This formulation is extended to SBP methods in multiple space dimensions on Cartesian and curvilinear meshes. Additional properties such as well-balancedness extend naturally from the underlying finite volume method to the SBP framework. We use the algorithmic construction enabled by the chosen formulation to derive several new entropy-preserving schemes for nonconservative hyperbolic systems, e.g., the compressible Euler equations of an ideal gas using the internal energy equation and a dispersive shallow-water model. Numerical experiments show the robustness and accuracy of the proposed schemes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes new entropy-preserving fluctuations for nonconservative hyperbolic systems that enable algorithmic construction of entropy-preserving methods, extending existing entropy-preserving techniques from conservative to nonconservative systems.
AI.motivation: Existing entropy-preserving methods for hyperbolic conservation laws rely on Tadmor's theory for two-point fluxes and SBP operators, but these have limitations for nonconservative systems. The authors aim to extend entropy-preserving techniques to nonconservative hyperbolic systems with simpler, more interpretable formulations.
AI.method: Proposes specific forms of entropy-preserving fluctuations for nonconservative systems, analyzes necessary/sufficient conditions, characterizes entropy-preserving three-point finite volume methods, and extends to SBP methods on Cartesian/curvilinear meshes in multiple dimensions.
AI.result: Develops algorithmic construction for entropy-preserving schemes for nonconservative systems, demonstrates new schemes for compressible Euler equations (internal energy equation) and dispersive shallow-water models, with numerical experiments showing robustness and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed formulation provides a systematic way to construct entropy-preserving methods for nonconservative hyperbolic systems, extending well-balanced properties from finite volume to SBP frameworks, with practical applications to important physical systems.

title: Measure preserving maps with bounded total variation
summary: Consider a piecewise affine Lipschitz map $φ: Ω\to \mathbb R$, where $Ω\subset \mathbb R^d$ is an open set, and assume that $x \mapsto x + t \nabla φ(x)$ is injective for almost every $t > 0$. In (J.-G. Liu, R.~L. Pego, \emph{Rigidly breaking potential flows and a countable Alexandrov theorem for polytopes}, Pure Appl. Anal., \textbf{7}(4), 2025) the authors conjecture that every such $φ$ must be locally convex. We prove the result assuming additionally $\nabla φ\in BV_{loc}(Ω)$, for a more general class of measure preserving maps.

AI.tldr: The paper proves a partial result supporting a conjecture about piecewise affine Lipschitz maps: if φ is such a map with ∇φ∈BV_loc and x↦x+t∇φ(x) is injective for almost every t>0, then φ must be locally convex.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to investigate the conjecture by Liu and Pego (2025) that piecewise affine Lipschitz maps with certain injectivity properties must be locally convex. This conjecture relates to rigidity properties of potential flows and Alexandrov-type theorems for polytopes.
AI.method: The authors prove the result under the additional assumption that ∇φ∈BV_loc(Ω) (bounded variation locally). They work with a more general class of measure preserving maps, extending beyond the specific conjecture's setting.
AI.result: The main result is a proof that under the additional BV_loc assumption on the gradient, the conjecture holds: every such piecewise affine Lipschitz map φ must be locally convex.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides partial confirmation of the Liu-Pego conjecture, showing it holds when the gradient has bounded variation locally. This contributes to understanding rigidity properties of measure-preserving maps and convexity conditions.

title: A bilinear inverse problem with forward operator inaccuracy applied to neonatal atlas-based diffuse optical tomography
summary: Linear inverse problems are highly common in practical real-world applications from industry to medical imaging. The forward operator is often built on some approximations of the studied system. Handling inaccuracies in the forward operator in the context of inverse problems is a relatively unstudied problem. In this work, we assume that we have a set of candidate forward operator matrices and suggest principal component analysis (PCA) for modeling their variation from the mean. We combine the original linear problem with the included forward operator inaccuracy into a bilinear tensor inverse problem and present two optimization algorithms and Gibbs sampling for approximately solving the problem. As a real-world test case, we apply the algorithms to account for the inaccuracy that is present in the sensitivity profiles or Jacobian matrices in diffuse optical tomography when an atlas-based model of the head anatomy is used instead of the subject's own anatomical model in neonates over a wide range of gestational ages (29--44 weeks). We report visual and numerical improvements in the spatial localization and contrast-to-noise-ratio in reconstructions of simulated hemodynamic activity.

AI.tldr: PCA-based approach for handling forward operator inaccuracies in linear inverse problems, applied to diffuse optical tomography in neonatal brain imaging.
AI.motivation: Linear inverse problems often use approximate forward operators, but handling these inaccuracies is understudied. Real-world applications like medical imaging need robust methods to account for forward model errors.
AI.method: Use PCA to model variation in candidate forward operator matrices, combine original linear problem with forward operator inaccuracy into bilinear tensor inverse problem, develop two optimization algorithms and Gibbs sampling for solution.
AI.result: Applied to diffuse optical tomography in neonates (29-44 weeks gestational age), showing visual and numerical improvements in spatial localization and contrast-to-noise-ratio for simulated hemodynamic activity reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PCA-based approach effectively handles forward operator inaccuracies in inverse problems, demonstrating practical improvements in neonatal brain imaging with atlas-based anatomical models.

title: Optimal Bilinear control restricted to the three-dimensional chemo-repulsion model with potential production
summary:
In this paper we study the following three-dimensional parabolic-parabolic chemo-repulsion model with potential production, logistic reaction and bilinear control, defined in $Q=(0,T)	imesΩ$:  egin{equation*}\label{eq0} \left\{  egin{array}{rcl} \partial_tu-Δu&=&
abla\cdot(u
abla v)+r\,u-μ\, u^p,\ \partial_tv-Δv+v&=&u^p+f\,v\, 1_{Ω_c}, \end{array} ight. \end{equation*} where $1< p<+\infty$, $r,μ\geq 0$, and $f=f(t,x)$ is the control function acting on a subdomain $(0,T)	imes Ω_c $, with $Ω_c\subseteqΩ$. This system is endowed with initial and non-flux boundary conditions. We prove the existence of global weak solutions of this controlled problem when $f\in L^{5/2}(0,T;L^{5/2}(Ω_c))$, analyzing the role of the diffusion and the logistic terms to get energy estimates. In particular, the logistic competition term $μ\, u^p$ is necessary only for $p>5/3$. Secondly, if $f\in L^{5/2}(0,T;L^{5/2+}(Ω_c))$, any weak solution $(u,v)$ satisfying the regularity criterion $u\in L^{5p/2}(Q)\cap L^{10/3}(Q)$ is in fact more regular, arriving in particular to $u,
abla v\in L^5(Q)$ for $p\le 2$ and $u,
abla v\in L^{5(p-1)}(Q)$ for $p> 2$ which is the critical regularity to solve a related optimal bilinear control problem.
  In fact, this setting let us to prove the existence of global optimal solutions, and the differentiability of the control-to-state mapping via the Implicit Function Theorem in Banach spaces. Then, we can identify the gradient of the (reduced) cost with respect to the control solving the adjoint problem
  by duality.
  In particular, we derive first-order necessary optimality conditions for local optimal solutions.

AI.tldr: Analysis of a 3D chemo-repulsion model with logistic growth and bilinear control, proving global weak solution existence, regularity criteria, and optimal control results including necessary optimality conditions.
AI.motivation: To study a controlled parabolic-parabolic chemo-repulsion system with logistic reaction terms, analyzing the interplay between diffusion, logistic competition, and bilinear control to establish solution existence, regularity, and optimal control theory.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of PDE system using energy estimates, regularity criteria, and functional analysis techniques including Implicit Function Theorem in Banach spaces and adjoint problem formulation for optimal control.
AI.result: Proved existence of global weak solutions for control in L^{5/2} spaces, established regularity criteria showing solutions gain higher regularity under certain conditions, and derived existence of global optimal solutions with first-order necessary optimality conditions.
AI.conclusion: The logistic competition term is crucial for p > 5/3, the system admits well-posed optimal control solutions, and the developed framework enables rigorous analysis of optimal bilinear control problems for chemo-repulsion models.

title: Complexity bounds on neural networks for the solution of structured linear systems of equations
summary: We derive upper bounds on the complexity of ReLU neural networks approximating the solution of a linear system given the matrix and the right-hand side. We focus on matrices which are symmetric positive definite and sparse, as they appear in the context of finite difference and finite element methods. For such matrices, we extend available results for the matrix inversion to the task of solving a linear system, where we leverage favorable properties of classical methods such as the modified Richardson and the conjugate gradient method. Our bounds on the number of layers and neurons are not only explicit with respect to the size of the matrices, but also with respect to their condition numbers.

AI.tldr: Upper bounds on ReLU neural network complexity for solving sparse SPD linear systems, with explicit dependence on matrix size and condition number.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to establish theoretical complexity bounds for neural networks solving linear systems, particularly for matrices arising in numerical methods like finite difference and finite element methods. This addresses the gap in understanding how well neural networks can approximate classical linear system solvers.
AI.method: Extends matrix inversion results to linear system solving by leveraging properties of classical iterative methods (modified Richardson and conjugate gradient). Focuses on symmetric positive definite sparse matrices and derives explicit bounds on network layers and neurons.
AI.result: Derives explicit upper bounds on the number of layers and neurons required for ReLU networks to approximate solutions of linear systems. Bounds are expressed in terms of both matrix size and condition number, showing favorable scaling properties.
AI.conclusion: Neural networks can efficiently approximate solutions to sparse SPD linear systems with complexity bounds that depend explicitly on matrix properties. The analysis connects classical numerical methods with neural network approximation theory, providing theoretical foundations for neural network-based linear solvers.

title: Well-posedness for the $\bar\partial$-problem relevant to the AKNS spectral problem
summary: The well-posedness for the Dbar problem associated with the AKNS spectral problem is considered. In general, the relevant Dbar equation with normalization condition is quivalent to an integral equation, where the kernel involves exponents $\mathrm{e}^{\pm2ikx}$ with physical variable $x$ as a parameter. We develop a decomposition technique to control the convergence of the integral by defining a new integral operator $RT_{\mathbb{C}}(k;x)$. The small norm condition of the operator is obtained to show that there exists a unique solution for the Dbar problem. Moreover, the Dbar dressing method is extended to construct the AKNS spectral problem and the potential construction is presented via the Dbar data. Prior estimates are given to show that the map from the Dbar data to the AKNS potential is Lipschitz continuous.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes well-posedness for the Dbar problem associated with AKNS spectral problem using decomposition techniques and small norm conditions, and extends Dbar dressing method to construct AKNS potentials with Lipschitz continuity.
AI.motivation: To address the well-posedness of the Dbar problem for AKNS spectral problems, which involves controlling convergence of integral equations with exponential kernels that depend on physical variable x.
AI.method: Develops decomposition technique to control integral convergence by defining new integral operator RT_ℂ(k;x), establishes small norm condition for operator existence, extends Dbar dressing method to construct AKNS spectral problem and potentials.
AI.result: Proves existence of unique solution for Dbar problem under small norm conditions, constructs AKNS spectral problem via Dbar data, shows Lipschitz continuity of map from Dbar data to AKNS potential through prior estimates.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully establishes well-posedness for AKNS Dbar problem, provides constructive method for AKNS potentials via Dbar dressing, and demonstrates Lipschitz continuity of the potential construction map.

title: Solving Maxwell's Equations with Mimetic Methods
summary: We present a mimetic finite-difference approach for solving Maxwell's equations in one and two spatial dimensions. After introducing the governing equations and the classical Finite-Difference Time-Domain (FDTD) method, we describe mimetic operators that satisfy a discrete analogue of the extended Gauss divergence theorem and show how they lead to a compact, physically consistent formulation for computational electromagnetics. Two numerical examples are presented: a one-dimensional sinusoidal wave interacting with a lossy dielectric slab, and a two-dimensional Gaussian pulse with Uniaxial Perfectly Matched Layer (UPML) absorbing boundary conditions. All implementations use the Mimetic Operators Library Enhanced (MOLE).

AI.tldr: Mimetic finite-difference method for solving Maxwell's equations in 1D/2D using discrete operators that preserve physical consistency, implemented with MOLE library.
AI.motivation: To develop a more physically consistent formulation for computational electromagnetics that preserves fundamental conservation laws through discrete analogues of continuum theorems.
AI.method: Mimetic finite-difference approach using operators that satisfy a discrete analogue of the extended Gauss divergence theorem, implemented with the Mimetic Operators Library Enhanced (MOLE).
AI.result: Successfully applied to two numerical examples: 1D sinusoidal wave interacting with lossy dielectric slab, and 2D Gaussian pulse with UPML absorbing boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: Mimetic operators provide a compact, physically consistent formulation for computational electromagnetics that preserves fundamental conservation laws at the discrete level.

title: A Sudakov Decomposition in Riemannian Manifolds with Positive Curvature
summary: In this paper, we study Monge's problem on Riemannian manifolds $(M, g)$ with positive sectional curvature. Assuming that the source and target measures are absolutely continuous with respect to the Riemannian volume measure, we generalize a variational method from the Euclidean setting to establish the existence of a transport density and an explicit disintegration of measures along optimal rays. These results extend the approach of Bianchini-Caravenna to the Riemannian context.

AI.tldr: Extends Monge's optimal transport problem to Riemannian manifolds with positive curvature, establishing existence of transport density and measure disintegration along optimal rays.
AI.motivation: To generalize Monge's optimal transport problem from Euclidean spaces to Riemannian manifolds with positive sectional curvature, extending the Bianchini-Caravenna approach to curved geometry.
AI.method: Generalizes a variational method from Euclidean optimal transport to Riemannian manifolds, assuming source and target measures are absolutely continuous with respect to Riemannian volume measure.
AI.result: Establishes existence of transport density and provides explicit disintegration of measures along optimal rays in Riemannian manifolds with positive sectional curvature.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extends the Bianchini-Caravenna variational approach to Riemannian geometry, providing tools for analyzing optimal transport on curved spaces with positive curvature.

title: A conservative, discontinuous Galerkin, tracer transport scheme using compatible finite elements
summary: This paper outlines a conservative transport scheme for scalar tracers within a compatible finite element model for geophysical fluid equations. Instead of using the advective transport equation for a mixing ratio, a conservative transport equation is solved for the tracer density of the mixing ratio multiplied by the dry density. This ensures mass conservation in the continuous equations, which can be preserved in the discrete equations with a discontinuous Galerkin transport scheme. Our method is designed to work for two placements of the mixing ratio in a Charney-Phillips vertical staggering: either co-located with the dry density or vertically staggered from it. The new scheme is designed to conserve the tracer density and ensure consistency by maintaining a constant mixing ratio. Additionally, a mass-conserving limiter is developed to ensure non-negativity in the co-located configuration. Tests with terminator toy chemistry and a moist rising bubble show the use of the new transport scheme with physics terms and its ability to accurately model mass conservation of moisture species in a dynamical core setup.

AI.tldr: A conservative transport scheme for scalar tracers in finite element geophysical fluid models that solves for tracer density instead of mixing ratio to ensure mass conservation, works with different vertical staggering placements, and includes a mass-conserving limiter for non-negativity.
AI.motivation: To develop a mass-conserving transport scheme for scalar tracers in geophysical fluid models that maintains conservation properties in both continuous and discrete equations, particularly important for accurate modeling of moisture species and chemical tracers in atmospheric simulations.
AI.method: Uses a conservative transport equation for tracer density (mixing ratio × dry density) instead of advective transport equation for mixing ratio alone. Implements discontinuous Galerkin transport scheme to preserve mass conservation discretely. Supports both co-located and vertically staggered placements in Charney-Phillips staggering. Includes a mass-conserving limiter for non-negativity in co-located configuration.
AI.result: The scheme successfully conserves tracer density and maintains constant mixing ratio consistency. Tests with terminator toy chemistry and moist rising bubble demonstrate accurate mass conservation of moisture species in dynamical core setups with physics terms.
AI.conclusion: The proposed conservative transport scheme effectively ensures mass conservation for scalar tracers in compatible finite element geophysical fluid models, working with different vertical staggering configurations while maintaining consistency and non-negativity through appropriate limiters.

title: Second order estimates for equations with sums of Hessian operators on Hermitian manifolds
summary: In this paper, we establish an a priori second-order estimate for admissible solutions satisfying a dynamic plurisubharmonic condition to equations involving sums of Hessian operators on compact Hermitian manifolds. The estimate is derived using a concavity inequality for complex sum-of-Hessian operators.

AI.tldr: A priori second-order estimate for admissible solutions to Hessian operator equations on compact Hermitian manifolds under dynamic plurisubharmonic conditions.
AI.motivation: To establish regularity estimates for solutions to complex Hessian equations on Hermitian manifolds, which are important in complex geometry and PDE theory.
AI.method: Uses a concavity inequality for complex sum-of-Hessian operators to derive the second-order estimate for admissible solutions satisfying dynamic plurisubharmonic conditions.
AI.result: Successfully establishes an a priori second-order estimate for admissible solutions to equations involving sums of Hessian operators on compact Hermitian manifolds.
AI.conclusion: The concavity inequality technique provides an effective approach for obtaining regularity estimates for complex Hessian equations in the Hermitian manifold setting.

title: Reduced order computation of 2D elastodynamic Green's functions in layered soil using a low-rank tensor approximation
summary: The evaluation of elastodynamic Green's functions across numerous source-receiver locations, frequencies, and material properties, particularly in the context of parametric studies or boundary element computations, is computationally demanding and memory intensive. This paper presents a reduced order modeling strategy based on the Greedy Tucker Approximation (GTA), which incrementally constructs a low-rank representation of the Green's tensor through rank-one enrichments obtained via a Proper Generalized Decomposition (PGD)-type alternating least squares procedure. A Petrov-Galerkin formulation is employed to improve convergence and approximation accuracy. The resulting multi-dimensional tensor, expressed in terms of one-dimensional basis functions and a compact core, achieves substantial reductions in memory requirements. The methodology is demonstrated for two cases: a soil layer on rigid bedrock and a layered halfspace. Different separable dimensions are considered to capture various combinations of source and receiver configurations, frequencies, and material parameters. Results are validated against those obtained with the direct stiffness method and computation times and memory requirements are compared.

AI.tldr: A reduced order modeling approach using Greedy Tucker Approximation with PGD-type enrichment for efficient computation of elastodynamic Green's functions across multiple parameters.
AI.motivation: Computing elastodynamic Green's functions for many source-receiver locations, frequencies, and material properties is computationally expensive and memory intensive, especially for parametric studies and boundary element methods.
AI.method: Greedy Tucker Approximation (GTA) with rank-one enrichments via Proper Generalized Decomposition (PGD)-type alternating least squares, using Petrov-Galerkin formulation for improved convergence and accuracy.
AI.result: The method achieves substantial memory reduction through multi-dimensional tensor representation with 1D basis functions and compact core. Validated against direct stiffness method for soil layer on rigid bedrock and layered halfspace cases.
AI.conclusion: The GTA-based reduced order modeling strategy provides an efficient approach for computing elastodynamic Green's functions with significant computational and memory savings while maintaining accuracy.

title: Well-posedness for the Navier-Stokes equations in Morrey spaces on non-compact manifolds
summary: We analyze the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations on a class of non-compact Riemannian manifolds within the framework of Morrey spaces. Assuming bounded geometry together with negative Ricci and sectional curvature (e.g., hyperbolic spaces), we establish dispersive and smoothing estimates for the heat semigroups associated with the Beltrami, Bochner and Hodge Laplacians in Morrey spaces, as well as for the Riesz transform. In particular, the presence of negative curvature yields improved large-time decay compared to the Euclidean setting. These estimates are of independent interest and enable us to construct solutions in time-weighted spaces of Kato type, leading to local-in-time well-posedness on a broad class of non-compact manifolds and global one in the case of Einstein manifolds. In the latter setting, we assume a smallness condition on the initial data in Morrey norms, which are weaker than $L^{p}$-norms and thus allow for certain classes of large $L^{p}$-data. We also discuss extensions to Ricci-flat manifolds. Our results introduce a new class of non-decaying and rough initial data for the Navier-Stokes equations on manifolds, extending previous works in Lebesgue and Sobolev spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes well-posedness for Navier-Stokes equations on non-compact Riemannian manifolds with negative curvature using Morrey spaces, allowing for rough non-decaying initial data.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of Navier-Stokes equations beyond Euclidean spaces and classical Lebesgue/Sobolev spaces to non-compact Riemannian manifolds with negative curvature, allowing for more general initial data that doesn't necessarily decay at infinity.
AI.method: Uses Morrey spaces framework on manifolds with bounded geometry and negative curvature. Establishes dispersive and smoothing estimates for heat semigroups (Beltrami, Bochner, Hodge Laplacians) and Riesz transform in Morrey spaces. Constructs solutions in time-weighted Kato-type spaces.
AI.result: Proves local-in-time well-posedness on broad class of non-compact manifolds and global well-posedness on Einstein manifolds with small initial data in Morrey norms (weaker than L^p norms, allowing certain large L^p data). Shows improved large-time decay due to negative curvature compared to Euclidean case.
AI.conclusion: Introduces new class of non-decaying and rough initial data for Navier-Stokes on manifolds, extending previous works. Results apply to hyperbolic spaces and can be extended to Ricci-flat manifolds, providing a more general framework than traditional Lebesgue/Sobolev space approaches.

title: GLENN: Neural network-enhanced computation of Ginzburg-Landau energy minimizers
summary: In this work, we propose a neural network-enhanced finite element strategy to compute the minimizer of the Ginzburg--Landau energy based on an unsupervised deep Ritz-type strategy. We treat the parameter $κ$ as a variable input parameter to obtain possible minimizers for a large range of $κ$-values. This allows for two possible strategies: 1) The neural network may be extensively trained to work as a stand-alone solver. 2) Neural network results are used as starting values for a subsequent classical iterative minimization procedure. The latter strategy particularly circumvents the missing reliability of the neural network-based approach. Numerical examples are presented that show the potential of the proposed strategy.

AI.tldr: Neural network-enhanced finite element method for Ginzburg-Landau energy minimization using unsupervised deep Ritz strategy with κ as variable input parameter.
AI.motivation: To develop a more efficient computational strategy for finding minimizers of the Ginzburg-Landau energy across a wide range of κ values, addressing reliability concerns in neural network-based approaches.
AI.method: Unsupervised deep Ritz-type neural network strategy integrated with finite elements, treating κ as variable input. Two strategies: 1) neural network as stand-alone solver, 2) neural network results as initialization for classical iterative minimization.
AI.result: Numerical examples demonstrate the potential of the proposed strategy for computing Ginzburg-Landau energy minimizers across different κ values.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid neural network-classical approach provides an effective strategy for Ginzburg-Landau minimization, with neural networks serving either as stand-alone solvers or as reliable initialization tools for traditional methods.

title: A Variational Approach to Degenerate Monge--Ampère Equations with Mixed Measures and Monotonicity
summary: We study the solvability and uniqueness for several degenerate Monge--Ampère equations including the Monge--Ampère eigenvalue problem in real Euclidean spaces that involve singular Borel measures. Our approach systematically analyzes the Monge--Ampère energy from the variational point of view and appropriately exploits monotonicity arguments. Our main tools consist of the mixed Monge--Ampère measure, Aleksandrov--Blocki--Jerison-type maximum principles, integration by parts, convex envelope, and comparison principles for subcritical equations. For the Monge--Ampère eigenvalue problem, we contrast the analysis within and without the energy class; even if it might not have solutions in the energy class, we show that the infimum of the Rayleigh quotient can be approximated from above by Monge--Ampère eigenvalues of the truncated measures, and by Rayleigh quotients of an inverse iterative scheme. We give examples showing that for very singular Borel measures, the Monge--Ampère eigenvalue problem has only solutions outside the energy class together with symmetry breaking and nonuniqueness.

AI.tldr: The paper studies solvability and uniqueness of degenerate Monge-Ampère equations with singular Borel measures, including the Monge-Ampère eigenvalue problem, using variational methods and monotonicity arguments.
AI.motivation: To understand the solvability and uniqueness properties of degenerate Monge-Ampère equations involving singular Borel measures, particularly the Monge-Ampère eigenvalue problem, which presents challenges when measures are very singular.
AI.method: Variational analysis of Monge-Ampère energy with monotonicity arguments, using mixed Monge-Ampère measure, Aleksandrov-Blocki-Jerison-type maximum principles, integration by parts, convex envelope, and comparison principles for subcritical equations.
AI.result: For very singular Borel measures, the Monge-Ampère eigenvalue problem may only have solutions outside the energy class, with symmetry breaking and nonuniqueness. The infimum of the Rayleigh quotient can be approximated from above by eigenvalues of truncated measures and by Rayleigh quotients of an inverse iterative scheme.
AI.conclusion: The study provides systematic analysis of degenerate Monge-Ampère equations with singular measures, revealing that solutions may exist outside the energy class for very singular measures, with important implications for uniqueness and symmetry properties.

title: Numerical Considerations for the Construction of Karhunen-Loève Expansions
summary: This report examines numerical aspects of constructing Karhunen-Loève expansions (KLEs) for second-order stochastic processes. The KLE relies on the spectral decomposition of the covariance operator via the Fredholm integral equation of the second kind, which is then discretized on a computational grid, leading to an eigendecomposition task. We derive the algebraic equivalence between this Fredholm-based eigensolution and the singular value decomposition of the weight-scaled sample matrix, yielding consistent solutions for both model-based and data-driven KLE construction. Analytical eigensolutions for exponential and squared-exponential covariance kernels serve as reference benchmarks to assess numerical consistency and accuracy in 1D settings. The convergence of SVD-based eigenvalue estimates and of the empirical distributions of the KL coefficients to their theoretical $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ target are characterized as a function of sample count. Higher-dimensional configurations include a two-dimensional irregular domain discretized by unstructured triangular meshes with two refinement levels, and a three-dimensional toroidal domain whose non-simply-connected topology motivates a comparison between Euclidean and shortest interior path distances between the grid points. The numerical results highlight the interplay between the discretization strategy, quadrature rule, and sample count, and their impact on the KLE results.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes numerical methods for constructing Karhunen-Loève expansions (KLEs) for stochastic processes, comparing Fredholm equation discretization with SVD approaches, and examining convergence, accuracy, and implementation challenges across 1D, 2D, and 3D domains.
AI.motivation: To develop robust numerical methods for constructing Karhunen-Loève expansions (KLEs) for second-order stochastic processes, addressing the computational challenges of discretizing Fredholm integral equations and ensuring accuracy across different domain geometries and covariance kernels.
AI.method: The study establishes algebraic equivalence between Fredholm-based eigendecomposition and SVD of weight-scaled sample matrices. It uses analytical solutions for exponential and squared-exponential kernels as benchmarks, examines convergence with sample count, and tests methods on 1D, 2D (unstructured triangular meshes), and 3D toroidal domains with different distance metrics.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates consistent solutions between Fredholm-based and SVD approaches, characterizes convergence of eigenvalue estimates and KL coefficient distributions to theoretical targets, and reveals the interplay between discretization strategy, quadrature rules, and sample count on KLE accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Numerical construction of KLEs requires careful consideration of discretization methods, quadrature rules, and sample sizes, with the choice between Euclidean vs. shortest-path distances becoming important in complex geometries like toroidal domains.

title: Remarks on Brunn-Minkowski-type inequalities related to the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator
summary: We investigate Brunn-Minkowski-type inequalities for the torsional rigidity $T_γ$ and the first eigenvalue $λ_γ$ associated with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator. Counterexamples are provided showing that neither concavity nor convexity properties hold for $T_γ$ on general bounded convex sets. We also demonstrate that log-concavity and log-convexity properties fail in this setting. In the case of centrally symmetric sets, we answer a question raised by Cordero-Erausquin and Eskenazis by showing that $T_γ^{1/(n+2)}$ is neither convex nor concave. On the positive side, we prove that $T_γ^{1/3}$ is convex with respect to Minkowski addition when restricted to Euclidean balls centered at the origin. For $λ_γ$, we answer negatively a question posed by Colesanti, Francini, Livshyts, and Salani by showing that the inequality $λ_γ(Ω_t)^{-1/2} \geq (1-t)λ_γ(Ω_0)^{-1/2} + tλ_γ(Ω_1)^{-1/2}$ does not hold, even for centrally symmetric sets.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates Brunn-Minkowski-type inequalities for torsional rigidity and first eigenvalue of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator, finding mostly negative results with one positive exception for Euclidean balls.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometric properties of torsional rigidity and first eigenvalue under the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator, specifically investigating whether they satisfy Brunn-Minkowski-type inequalities (concavity/convexity properties) with respect to Minkowski addition.
AI.method: The authors provide counterexamples to disprove various conjectured inequalities, construct specific examples showing failure of concavity/convexity properties, and prove one positive result using analytical methods for the special case of Euclidean balls.
AI.result: Most results are negative: neither concavity nor convexity holds for torsional rigidity on general convex sets; log-concavity/log-convexity also fail; for centrally symmetric sets, T_γ^{1/(n+2)} is neither convex nor concave; λ_γ^{-1/2} inequality fails even for centrally symmetric sets. The only positive result: T_γ^{1/3} is convex for Euclidean balls centered at origin.
AI.conclusion: Brunn-Minkowski-type inequalities generally fail for torsional rigidity and first eigenvalue of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck operator, with the exception of a specific convexity property for torsional rigidity restricted to Euclidean balls. The paper resolves several open questions in the negative.

title: SIREN Residual Error as a Regularity Diagnostic for Navier-Stokes Equations
summary: We introduce a method for detecting regularity loss in solutions to the three-dimensional Navier-Stokes equations using the approximation error of Sinusoidal Representation Networks (SIRENs). SIRENs use sin() activations, producing C-infinity outputs that cannot represent non-smooth features. By classical spectral approximation theory, the SIREN error is bounded by O(N^{-s}) where s is the local Sobolev regularity. At a singularity (s to 0), the error is O(1) and localizes via the Gibbs phenomenon. We decompose the velocity field into a cheap analytical baseline (advection-diffusion) and a learned residual (pressure correction), training a compact SIREN (4,867 parameters). We validate on the 3D Taylor-Green vortex, where error concentration increases from 4.9x to 13.6x as viscosity decreases from 0.01 to 0.0001, localizing to the stagnation point -- the geometry matching the singularity proven by Chen and Hou (2025) for 3D Euler. On axisymmetric equations, we reproduce blowup signatures (T* converging across resolutions) and identify a critical viscosity nu_c = 0.00582 for the regularization transition.

AI.tldr: SIREN neural networks detect regularity loss in 3D Navier-Stokes solutions by exploiting their inability to represent non-smooth features, with error concentration increasing as viscosity decreases and localizing to known singularity points.
AI.motivation: To develop a method for detecting potential singularities and regularity loss in 3D Navier-Stokes equations, which remains a major open problem in fluid dynamics, using neural networks as diagnostic tools.
AI.method: Use Sinusoidal Representation Networks (SIRENs) with sin() activations that produce smooth outputs. Decompose velocity field into analytical baseline (advection-diffusion) and learned residual (pressure correction). Train compact SIRENs and analyze approximation error, which becomes O(1) at singularities due to Gibbs phenomenon.
AI.result: Error concentration increases from 4.9x to 13.6x as viscosity decreases from 0.01 to 0.0001 in 3D Taylor-Green vortex, localizing to stagnation point matching proven singularity geometry. On axisymmetric equations, reproduce blowup signatures and identify critical viscosity nu_c = 0.00582 for regularization transition.
AI.conclusion: SIRENs provide an effective diagnostic tool for detecting regularity loss in 3D Navier-Stokes equations, with error localization identifying potential singularity locations and critical viscosity thresholds for blowup behavior.

title: Nonlinear Incompressible Shear Wave Models in Hyperelasticity and Viscoelasticity Frameworks, with Applications to Love Waves
summary: General equations describing shear displacements in incompressible hyperelastic materials, holding for an arbitrary form of strain energy density function, are presented and applied to the description of nonlinear Love-type waves propagating on an interface between materials with different mechanical properties. The model is valid for a broad class of hyper-viscoelastic materials. For a cubic Yeoh model, shear wave equations contain cubic and quintic differential polynomial terms, including viscoelasticity contributions in terms of dispersion terms that include mixed derivatives $u_{xxt}$ of the material displacement. Full (2+1)-dimensional numerical simulations of waves propagating in the bulk of a two-layered solid are undertaken and analyzed with respect to the source position and mechanical properties of the layers. Interfacial nonlinear Love waves and free upper surface shear waves are tracked; it is demonstrated that in the fully nonlinear case, the variable wave speed of interface and surface waves generally satisfies the linear Love wave existence condition $c_1 < \abs{v} < c_2$, while tending to the larger material wave speed $c_1$ or $c_2$ for large times.

AI.tldr: General equations for shear displacements in incompressible hyperelastic materials are derived and applied to nonlinear Love-type waves at material interfaces, with numerical simulations showing wave behavior in two-layered solids.
AI.motivation: To develop a general framework for describing shear displacements and nonlinear Love-type waves in hyperelastic materials that works for arbitrary strain energy density functions, extending beyond linear elastic theory to capture nonlinear wave phenomena at material interfaces.
AI.method: Derived general equations for shear displacements in incompressible hyperelastic materials valid for arbitrary strain energy density functions. Applied these to nonlinear Love-type waves at interfaces between materials with different properties. For the cubic Yeoh model, obtained shear wave equations with cubic and quintic differential polynomial terms including viscoelastic dispersion terms. Conducted full (2+1)-dimensional numerical simulations of wave propagation in two-layered solids, analyzing effects of source position and material properties.
AI.result: For the cubic Yeoh model, shear wave equations contain cubic and quintic differential polynomial terms with viscoelastic dispersion contributions. Numerical simulations tracked interfacial nonlinear Love waves and free upper surface shear waves. In fully nonlinear cases, variable wave speeds generally satisfy the linear Love wave existence condition c₁ < |v| < c₂, while tending toward the larger material wave speed (c₁ or c₂) for large times.
AI.conclusion: The developed general framework successfully describes nonlinear Love-type waves in hyperelastic materials, with numerical simulations confirming that nonlinear wave behavior at interfaces generally respects the linear Love wave existence condition while exhibiting time-dependent wave speed evolution toward material limits.

title: The Geometry of Polycons and a Counterexample to Wachspress' Conjecture
summary:
Polycons, initially introduced by Wachspress in 1975 as a tool in finite element methods, are generalizations of polygons in that they allow conic boundary components. We are interested in the adjoint curve of a given polycon, i.e. the unique curve of minimal degree vanishing in the so-called residual arrangement. It was conjectured by Wachspress that under some regularity assumptions this curve does not vanish in the interior of its defining polycon. However, until recently the only class of polycons for which this was proven were convex polygons. We present a polycon bounded by three conics that constitutes a counterexample to Wachspress' conjecture.
  The origin of this counterexample reveals some beautiful geometry of polycons. Replacing one degree two boundary component of a polycon with a line produces a new polycon. We show that the adjoint of the latter is a contact curve to the adjoint of the former. This naturally leads to the consideration of symmetric linear determinantal representations of adjoints, which lets us explicitly describe the fibers of the adjoint map in the case of polycons bounded by three conics. As a corollary we prove that generically the adjoint of a polycon bounded by three conics is smooth.

AI.tldr: A polycon bounded by three conics provides a counterexample to Wachspress' conjecture that the adjoint curve doesn't vanish in the interior of its defining polycon.
AI.motivation: To investigate Wachspress' 1975 conjecture about adjoint curves of polycons (generalized polygons with conic boundaries), which had only been proven for convex polygons, and to understand the geometry of polycons.
AI.method: Study the geometry of polycons by replacing degree two boundary components with lines, showing adjoints become contact curves. Use symmetric linear determinantal representations of adjoints to explicitly describe fibers of the adjoint map for polycons bounded by three conics.
AI.result: Found a polycon bounded by three conics that serves as a counterexample to Wachspress' conjecture. Proved that generically the adjoint of a polycon bounded by three conics is smooth.
AI.conclusion: Wachspress' conjecture is false for general polycons, as demonstrated by a counterexample with three conic boundaries. The study reveals rich geometry connecting polycons, adjoint curves, and determinantal representations.

title: Contrasting behaviour of two spherically symmetric perfect fluids near a weak null singularity in a spherically symmetric black hole
comment: 85 pages, 18 figures. The conference proceedings paper 'Reviewing two perfect fluids near a weak null singularity in spherical symmetry', submitted for the proceedings of the GR24-Amaldi16 conference proceedings, and accepted for publication in February 2026, is based on the current paper
summary: In this work we contrast the behaviour of two spherically symmetric matter models in a class of spherically symmetric spacetimes which feature a weak null singularity. This class in particular contains spherically symmetric perturbations of subextremal Reissner-Nordström under the Einstein--Maxwell--scalar field system, a system for which a $C^2$ formulation of the strong cosmic censorship conjecture was proved by Luk-Oh, arXiv:1702.05715 and Dafermos, arXiv:1201.1797. Firstly, we consider the Cauchy problem of spherically symmetric dust falling into the weak null singularity (WNS) where the initial dust velocity is normal to a smooth spacelike curve with certain properties. We prove that the flow of the dust velocity does not experience any shell-crossing before or at the singularity, the velocity vector remains timelike, and that the dust energy density remains bounded as matter approaches the singularity. Secondly, we consider the characteristic initial value problem for stiff perfect fluid falling into the WNS. By relating the stiff fluid velocity and energy density to a scalar field satisfying the homogeneous linear wave equation, we prove that this energy density becomes infinite as we approach the weak null singularity. Furthermore, we show that the ingoing component of the stiff fluid velocity blows up while the outgoing component approaches zero at the singularity. Therefore the velocity vector approaches an ingoing null vector tangent to the singular hypersurface.

AI.tldr: Two matter models (dust and stiff fluid) falling into a weak null singularity are analyzed, showing dust energy density remains bounded while stiff fluid energy density blows up at the singularity.
AI.motivation: To understand how different matter models behave when falling into weak null singularities, particularly in the context of spherically symmetric perturbations of Reissner-Nordström black holes where strong cosmic censorship has been proven.
AI.method: 1) Cauchy problem for spherically symmetric dust falling into WNS with initial velocity normal to smooth spacelike curve. 2) Characteristic initial value problem for stiff perfect fluid falling into WNS, relating stiff fluid to scalar field satisfying homogeneous linear wave equation.
AI.result: Dust: no shell-crossing, velocity remains timelike, energy density bounded at singularity. Stiff fluid: energy density becomes infinite, ingoing velocity component blows up, outgoing component approaches zero, velocity approaches ingoing null vector tangent to singular hypersurface.
AI.conclusion: Different matter models exhibit fundamentally different behavior at weak null singularities - dust remains well-behaved while stiff fluid becomes singular, showing matter model choice significantly affects physical predictions near such singularities.

title: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow for Transition Probability Density Functions of Diffusion Models
summary: We propose a new Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow framework to approximate the transition probability density function of a diffusion process by solving the corresponding Fokker-Planck equation with an atomic initial distribution, parametrically with respect to the location of the initial mass. By using Normalizing Flows, we look for the solution as a transformation of the transition probability density function of a reference stochastic process, ensuring that our approximation is structure-preserving and automatically satisfies positivity and mass conservation constraints. By extending Neural Galerkin schemes to the context of Normalizing Flows, we derive a system of ODEs for the time evolution of the Normalizing Flow's parameters. Adaptive sampling routines are used to evaluate the Fokker-Planck residual in meaningful locations, which is of vital importance to address high-dimensional PDEs. Numerical results show that this strategy captures key features of the true solution and enforces the causal relationship between the initial datum and the density function at subsequent times. After completing an offline training phase, online evaluation becomes significantly more cost-effective than solving the PDE from scratch. The proposed method serves as a promising surrogate model, which could be deployed in many-query problems associated with stochastic differential equations, like Bayesian inference, simulation, and diffusion bridge generation.

AI.tldr: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow framework for approximating diffusion process transition densities by solving Fokker-Planck equations with adaptive sampling and offline training.
AI.motivation: To efficiently approximate transition probability density functions of diffusion processes for many-query problems like Bayesian inference, simulation, and diffusion bridge generation, where solving PDEs from scratch is computationally expensive.
AI.method: Combines Normalizing Flows with Neural Galerkin schemes: uses Normalizing Flows as structure-preserving transformations of reference process densities, derives ODEs for parameter evolution, employs adaptive sampling for residual evaluation, and implements offline training with online deployment.
AI.result: The method captures key features of true solutions, enforces causal relationships between initial data and subsequent densities, and provides significantly more cost-effective online evaluation compared to solving PDEs from scratch.
AI.conclusion: The framework serves as a promising surrogate model for many-query problems involving stochastic differential equations, offering structure-preserving approximations with automatic positivity and mass conservation constraints.

title: Quantifying the effect of noise perturbation for the stochastic Burgers equation with additive trace-class noise
summary: We establish upper bounds for the weak and strong error resulting from a perturbation of the noise driving the stochastic Burgers equation, where we assume the noise to be additive and of trace class and the initial value to be sufficiently regular. More specifically, replacing the covariance operator of the driving noise $Q_1 \in \mathcal{L}_1(L^2)$ in the Burgers equation by a covariance operator $Q_2 \in \mathcal{L}_1(L^2)$ results in a weak error of $\mathcal{O}\big(\| (-A)^{-1^{-} } (Q_1-Q_2) \|_{\mathcal{L}_1(L^2)}\big)$ and a strong error of $\mathcal{O}\big(\big\| (-A)^{-1/2^{-}}\big|Q_1^{1/2} -Q_2^{1/2}\big| \big\|_{\mathcal{L}_2(L^2)}\big)$. Here $\|\cdot \|_{\mathcal{L}_1}$ is the trace class norm, $\|\cdot \|_{\mathcal{L}_2}$ is the Hilbert-Schmidt norm, and $A$ is the one-dimensional Dirichlet Laplacian that represents the leading term in the Burgers equation. In particular, our results provide upper bounds for the weak and strong error arising when approximating the trace class noise by finite-dimensional noise; the rates we obtain reflect the general philosophy that the weak convergence rate should be twice the strong rate.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes upper bounds for weak and strong errors in the stochastic Burgers equation when perturbing the trace class additive noise covariance operator.
AI.motivation: To quantify how perturbations in the noise covariance operator affect solutions of the stochastic Burgers equation, particularly for understanding approximation errors when using finite-dimensional noise approximations.
AI.method: Analyzes the stochastic Burgers equation with additive trace class noise, replacing covariance operator Q₁ with Q₂, and derives error bounds using functional analysis tools including trace class and Hilbert-Schmidt norms with the Dirichlet Laplacian operator A.
AI.result: Obtained explicit upper bounds: weak error is O(‖(-A)^(-1⁻)(Q₁-Q₂)‖_L₁) and strong error is O(‖(-A)^(-1/2⁻)|Q₁^(1/2)-Q₂^(1/2)|‖_L₂), showing weak convergence rate is twice the strong rate.
AI.conclusion: The analysis provides rigorous error bounds for noise perturbations in stochastic Burgers equations, confirming the general principle that weak error convergence is twice as fast as strong error convergence, with applications to finite-dimensional noise approximations.

title: Homogeneous Sobolev gradient flow of the length functional
summary:
We study the gradient flow of the length functional on the space of planar immersed closed curves, where the gradient is taken with respect to a family of homogeneous Sobolev $H^1$-type Riemannian metrics depending on parameters $λ>0$ and $a\in\mathbb{R}$. The gradient can be written explicitly in terms of arc-length convolution with the periodic Green's function for the second-order operator associated with the $H^1$ metric, and then the gradient flow is a reparametrisation-invariant nonlocal ODE.
  Working in the optimal low-regularity setting $W^{1,1}(\mathbb{S},\mathbb{R}^2)$, we show that the gradient is locally Lipschitz to obtain local well-posedness via the Picard--Lindelöf theorem in Banach spaces. A time-reparametrisation reduces the analysis for general $a$ to the model case ${a=2}$, for which we obtain exponential decay of the length and global existence with uniform convergence in $W^{1,1}$ to a constant map. For $C^1$ immersed initial data we show that immersion is preserved for all time, and we further prove that if the initial curve bounds a convex set then convexity is also preserved by the flow.

AI.tldr: The paper studies gradient flow of length functional on planar closed curves under Sobolev H¹-type metrics, proving local well-posedness in low regularity, global existence with convergence to constant maps, and preservation of immersion and convexity.
AI.motivation: To understand the gradient flow of the length functional on planar immersed closed curves under a family of homogeneous Sobolev H¹-type Riemannian metrics, which provides a geometric evolution equation with reparametrization invariance and nonlocal properties.
AI.method: Uses explicit gradient formulation via arc-length convolution with periodic Green's function for second-order operator. Employs Picard-Lindelöf theorem in Banach spaces for local well-posedness in W^{1,1} setting. Uses time-reparametrization to reduce analysis to model case a=2. Proves preservation properties through analytical techniques.
AI.result: Local well-posedness in optimal low-regularity W^{1,1} setting. Exponential decay of length and global existence with uniform W^{1,1} convergence to constant map for model case a=2. Preservation of immersion for C¹ initial data. Preservation of convexity when initial curve bounds convex set.
AI.conclusion: The gradient flow under Sobolev H¹-type metrics provides a well-defined geometric evolution with strong analytical properties including local existence, global convergence, and preservation of important geometric features like immersion and convexity.

title: Quantum block encoding for semiseparable matrices
summary:
Quantum block encoding (QBE) is a crucial step in the development of most quantum algorithms, as it provides an embedding of a given matrix into a suitable larger unitary matrix. Historically, the development of efficient techniques for QBE has mostly focused on sparse matrices; less effort has been devoted to data-sparse (e.g., rank-structured) matrices.
  In this work we examine a particular case of rank structure, namely, one-pair semiseparable matrices. We present a new block encoding approach that relies on a suitable factorization of the given matrix as the product of triangular and diagonal factors. To encode the matrix, the algorithm needs $2\log(N)+7$ ancillary qubits. This process takes polylogarithmic time and has an error of $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$, where $N$ is the matrix size.

AI.tldr: New quantum block encoding method for one-pair semiseparable matrices using triangular-diagonal factorization with 2log(N)+7 ancilla qubits, polylog time, and O(N²) error.
AI.motivation: Quantum block encoding has focused mainly on sparse matrices, but less attention has been given to data-sparse/rank-structured matrices like semiseparable matrices, which are important for many applications.
AI.method: Factorizes one-pair semiseparable matrices as product of triangular and diagonal factors, then encodes using quantum circuit requiring only 2log(N)+7 ancillary qubits with polylogarithmic time complexity.
AI.result: Achieves efficient block encoding with minimal ancilla qubits (2log(N)+7), polylogarithmic time complexity, and error scaling as O(N²) where N is matrix size.
AI.conclusion: Provides first efficient quantum block encoding method specifically for one-pair semiseparable matrices, extending QBE capabilities beyond sparse matrices to important rank-structured matrix classes.

title: Radiation damping of the soliton internal mode in 1D quadratic Klein-Gordon equation
summary: We study long-time dynamics of small even perturbations of the soliton in 1D quadratic Klein-Gordon equation. The soliton possesses both an internal mode and the unstable mode. On a codimension-one manifold of fine-tuned initial data the instability is suppressed and the internal mode decays slowly by transferring energy into the continuum. We show that this decay and the associated nonlinear frequency shift are accurately captured by a cubic resonant approximation, with the damping rate determined by a Fermi golde rule-type coefficient. This provides a quantitative description of irreversible energy transfer from the internal mode to dispersive radiation.

AI.tldr: Small even perturbations of soliton in 1D quadratic Klein-Gordon equation exhibit suppressed instability on fine-tuned manifold, with internal mode decaying slowly via energy transfer to continuum, described by cubic resonant approximation with Fermi golden rule-type damping.
AI.motivation: To understand long-time dynamics of perturbations around solitons in nonlinear wave equations, particularly how internal modes decay and transfer energy to radiation in systems with both internal and unstable modes.
AI.method: Study small even perturbations of soliton in 1D quadratic Klein-Gordon equation, analyze codimension-one manifold of fine-tuned initial data where instability is suppressed, use cubic resonant approximation to capture decay and nonlinear frequency shift, apply Fermi golden rule-type coefficient for damping rate.
AI.result: On fine-tuned manifold, internal mode decays slowly by transferring energy to continuum radiation, with decay rate and nonlinear frequency shift accurately described by cubic resonant approximation, providing quantitative description of irreversible energy transfer.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a quantitative framework for understanding irreversible energy transfer from internal modes to dispersive radiation in perturbed soliton systems, with Fermi golden rule-type damping governing the decay process.

title: Index Estimates for CMC and Minimal Surfaces with Capillary Boundary
comment: 15 pages, with in addition 9 pages of appendix
summary: We prove that the index of a CMC surface with capillary boundary is bounded from above linearly by its genus, number of boundary components, and branching order, and also by some Willmore-type energy involving the area, mean curvature, contact angle, and ambient curvature. The main auxiliary theorem of more general interest is a comparison of the second variations of area and energy at a branched conformal map with boundary. In the appendix we derive the various second variation formulae for area, enclosed-volume, and wetting functionals away from critical points and for non-admissible variations, the purpose of which is to rather comprehensively fill a gap in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper proves an upper bound for the index of CMC surfaces with capillary boundaries in terms of genus, boundary components, branching order, and Willmore-type energy.
AI.motivation: To establish index bounds for constant mean curvature (CMC) surfaces with capillary boundary conditions, connecting geometric invariants like genus and boundary components to analytic quantities like Willmore energy.
AI.method: Main approach involves proving a comparison theorem for second variations of area and energy at branched conformal maps with boundary. The appendix provides comprehensive second variation formulae for area, enclosed-volume, and wetting functionals.
AI.result: The index of a CMC surface with capillary boundary is bounded linearly by its genus, number of boundary components, branching order, and by a Willmore-type energy involving area, mean curvature, contact angle, and ambient curvature.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes fundamental index bounds for capillary CMC surfaces and provides comprehensive second variation formulae that fill a gap in the literature, with the main auxiliary theorem having broader applications beyond the specific capillary boundary problem.

title: Rigorous Error Certification for Neural PDE Solvers: From Empirical Residuals to Solution Guarantees
summary:
Uncertainty quantification for partial differential equations is traditionally grounded in discretization theory, where solution error is controlled via mesh/grid refinement. Physics-informed neural networks fundamentally depart from this paradigm: they approximate solutions by minimizing residual losses at collocation points, introducing new sources of error arising from optimization, sampling, representation, and overfitting. As a result, the generalization error in the solution space remains an open problem.
  Our main theoretical contribution establishes generalization bounds that connect residual control to solution-space error. We prove that when neural approximations lie in a compact subset of the solution space, vanishing residual error guarantees convergence to the true solution. We derive deterministic and probabilistic convergence results and provide certified generalization bounds translating residual, boundary, and initial errors into explicit solution error guarantees.

AI.tldr: Theoretical analysis shows that for physics-informed neural networks, controlling residual error leads to solution convergence when neural approximations are in compact subsets of solution space.
AI.motivation: Traditional PDE uncertainty quantification relies on mesh refinement, but PINNs introduce new error sources (optimization, sampling, representation, overfitting). The generalization error in solution space for PINNs remains an open problem that needs theoretical understanding.
AI.method: Establishes theoretical generalization bounds connecting residual control to solution-space error. Proves that when neural approximations lie in compact subsets of solution space, vanishing residual error guarantees convergence to true solution. Derives both deterministic and probabilistic convergence results.
AI.result: Provides certified generalization bounds that translate residual, boundary, and initial errors into explicit solution error guarantees. Shows that residual error control leads to solution convergence under compactness conditions.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides theoretical foundation for PINN error analysis, establishing that solution-space convergence can be guaranteed through residual control when neural approximations satisfy compactness conditions, addressing the open problem of generalization error in PINNs.
